modules.title: Programming Basics with Java
modules.description: Your first month of real programming — variables, conditions, loops, and methods, all in Java. No CS background needed; if you can operate WhatsApp, you can start here.

topics.title: Hello, Java: your first program
topics.summary_md:
Every Java program starts with the same ritual: a class, a `main` method, and a `System.out.println` line. It looks like a lot of ceremony just to print one sentence — and honestly, it is. Don't try to decode every keyword on day one. Type it, run it, change the message, run it again. The words `public static void main` will make full sense by the end of this pathway; for now, treat them as the fixed opening line of every program you write.

First, get set up. Install a JDK (version 17 or newer) and an editor — IntelliJ IDEA Community or VS Code, both free. If the installation fights you, start on an online compiler like Programiz and install properly this weekend. Don't let setup trouble eat your first week; plenty of people quit Java at the installer, not at the code.

Here's your first real task: write a program that prints your name and your college on two separate lines, exactly like the front page of a new record notebook. Then break it on purpose — remove a semicolon, misspell `println` — and read the error messages. Compiler errors feel rude at first, but they're the most honest teacher you'll ever get: they tell you the exact line where things went wrong. Getting comfortable reading them is half the skill.
tips: Your file name must match your class name exactly — `Hello.java` needs `class Hello`. This bites every beginner once.
Don't memorise `public static void main` — you'll understand each word naturally as you learn OOP.
Read compiler errors from the top; the first error often causes all the ones below it.
Interviewers sometimes ask you to write Hello World on paper — practice writing it by hand once.

topics.title: JDK, JVM, bytecode — what happens when you hit Run
topics.summary_md:
You typed `Hello.java` and hit Run. What actually happened? Two steps. First, the compiler (`javac`) translated your file into *bytecode* — a `.class` file full of instructions no human reads. Second, the JVM (Java Virtual Machine) picked up that bytecode and executed it on your laptop.

Why the middle step? Because bytecode is the same everywhere. Think of it like a scanned PDF of your assignment: you make it once, and it opens the same on your friend's Windows laptop, the lab's ancient desktop, and a phone. The JVM on each machine does the local translation. That's the famous "write once, run anywhere" line — you compile once, and any device with a JVM can run it.

Three names get mixed up constantly, so pin them down now. The **JVM** runs bytecode. The **JRE** is the JVM plus the standard libraries — enough to *run* Java programs. The **JDK** is the JRE plus developer tools like the compiler — what you need to *write* Java. You installed the JDK; your users only need a JVM.

This topic won't change how you write loops tomorrow. But "explain JDK vs JRE vs JVM" shows up in almost every fresher interview and viva, and a clean thirty-second answer here sets the tone for the rest of the round.
tips: One line to remember: JDK builds it, JVM runs it, JRE is the runtime package in between.
"Is Java compiled or interpreted?" — it's both: compiled to bytecode, then executed by the JVM. Say exactly that.
You don't need to know JVM internals now; save garbage collection details for the OOP module.

topics.title: Variables, types, and the maths Java actually does
topics.summary_md:
A variable is a labelled box that holds one value. Java makes you declare what *kind* of value before you use it: `int` for whole numbers, `double` for decimals, `boolean` for true/false, `char` for a single character, and `String` for text. This strictness feels annoying coming from Python, but it catches your mistakes before the program even runs — you'll learn to like it.

Operators are the usual school maths — `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` — plus one you'll use constantly: `%` (modulo), which gives the remainder. Even or odd? Check `n % 2`. Last digit of a number? `n % 10`.

Now the trap that catches every single beginner. Say you attended 43 classes out of 60 and want your attendance percentage. You write `int percent = 43 / 60 * 100;` and Java prints **0**. Why? Because `43 / 60` is int divided by int, and Java throws away the decimal part — 0.716 becomes 0, and 0 × 100 is 0. Fix it by making one side a decimal: `43.0 / 60 * 100` gives 71.66. Integer division isn't a bug; it's a rule. Burn it in now and you'll dodge a hundred confusing moments later.

Also note: `char` uses single quotes (`'A'`), `String` uses double quotes (`"A"`). Java treats them as completely different things.
tips: `1 / 2` is 0 in Java, not 0.5 — int divided by int stays int. This one line causes more beginner confusion than anything else.
Name variables for what they hold: `totalMarks`, not `tm` or `x`.
`%` is the interview workhorse: even/odd checks, extracting digits, cycling through positions.
Written placement tests love asking what mixed int/double expressions evaluate to — practice a few by hand.

topics.title: Reading input and making decisions
topics.summary_md:
Programs get interesting when they react. Two pieces make that happen: `Scanner` to read what the user types, and `if / else` to choose what to do about it.

`Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);` then `sc.nextInt()` for numbers, `sc.nextLine()` for a full line of text. One famous trap: calling `nextInt()` and then `nextLine()` makes the second one grab the leftover Enter key and return an empty string. When your program "skips" an input, that's what happened — add an extra `sc.nextLine()` after the number to clear it.

Conditions are just questions with two exits. Here's a full example you can build right now: a grade printer for your semester marks. Read the marks, then — 90 and above prints `O grade`, 80–89 prints `A+`, below 50 prints `Arrear, register for revaluation`. Chain the checks with `if / else if / else`, and put the biggest cutoff first; order matters, because Java takes the *first* branch that matches and skips the rest.

Two comparison rules to tattoo somewhere: use `==` for numbers, but **`.equals()` for Strings** — `==` on Strings compares whether they're the same object in memory, not the same text. And `=` assigns while `==` compares; mixing them up in a condition won't even compile in Java, which is the language quietly saving you.

For fixed menus ("press 1 for mess, 2 for canteen"), `switch` reads cleaner than a ladder of ifs — just don't forget `break` after each case.
tips: String comparison uses `.equals()`, never `==`. Interviewers deliberately test this.
`nextInt()` followed by `nextLine()` skips input — clear the leftover newline with an extra `nextLine()`.
In an if–else-if ladder, order your conditions from most specific to least; the first match wins.
A `switch` case without `break` falls through to the next case — a classic written-test question.

topics.title: Loops: making the computer do the boring part
topics.summary_md:
A loop repeats work so you don't have to. Java gives you three flavours. `for` when you know how many times ("print 1 to 100"). `while` when you repeat until something changes ("keep asking until the input is valid"). `do-while` when the body must run at least once ("show the menu, then ask again").

The anatomy of `for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)`: start at 0, keep going while `i < 5`, add 1 each round. That runs exactly 5 times — for i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4. Counting from 0 feels odd for a week and then becomes second nature.

Here's the exercise that made loops click for generations of students: star patterns, the same ones you've drawn in lab record margins. Print this with a loop inside a loop:

```
*
* *
* * *
* * * *
```

The outer loop picks the row (1 to 4); the inner loop prints that many stars. Nested loops are exactly this — for every round of the outer one, the inner one runs fully. Service-company written tests (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) genuinely still ask pattern questions, so this isn't just a toy.

When a loop misbehaves, don't stare — **dry run** it: take paper, make a column for each variable, and walk through every iteration by hand. Five minutes of dry running teaches more than an hour of guessing. And if your program hangs forever, you almost certainly forgot the line that changes the loop variable.
tips: Off-by-one errors live at the boundaries — always check your loop's first and last iteration by hand.
Forgot `i++` in a while loop? That's your infinite loop. It happens to everyone; check it first.
Dry run on paper before you run on the machine — interviewers watch for this habit in person too.
Pattern-printing questions are still standard in service-company placement tests. Do ten of them and they stop being scary.

topics.title: Methods: name a chunk of work, reuse it forever
topics.summary_md:
A method is a named block of code you can call whenever you need it. Instead of copy-pasting the same ten lines in three places, you write them once, give them a name, and call the name. Three parts matter: the **name**, the **parameters** (what goes in), and the **return type** (what comes out — or `void` if nothing does).

Concrete example: a mess-bill calculator. Write `int messBill(int days, int ratePerDay)` that returns `days * ratePerDay`. Now the monthly bill is `messBill(26, 80)`, your friend's partial month is `messBill(11, 80)`, and if the mess rate changes next semester you edit exactly one place. That "edit one place" feeling is why methods exist.

One rule that will confuse you later if you skip it now: **Java always passes a copy of the value**. If a method receives an `int` and changes it, the caller's variable doesn't move. Write a `bump(int x)` method that adds 10, call it, print the original — it's unchanged. Prove it to yourself today; the interview version of this question ("is Java pass-by-value or pass-by-reference?") has one correct answer: pass-by-value, always.

You'll also meet **overloading**: two methods with the same name but different parameter lists, like `area(int side)` for a square and `area(int l, int b)` for a rectangle. Java picks the right one by looking at the arguments. Same name, different inputs — that's overloading, and it's a favourite viva question.
tips: One method, one job. If you're describing your method with the word 'and', split it.
Java is pass-by-value, always — even for objects (the reference itself is copied). Memorise the phrasing now.
Name methods as verbs: `calculateBill()`, `printReceipt()` — code should read like instructions.
Overloading (same name, different parameters) vs overriding comes later — but interviewers pair them, so note the word now.

modules.title: Core Java & OOP
modules.description: Objects, inheritance, interfaces, and the String questions every fresher interview asks. This module is where Java stops being syntax and starts being a way of thinking.

topics.title: Classes and objects: the blueprint and the thing
topics.summary_md:
A class is a blueprint; an object is one real thing built from it. That sentence gets repeated everywhere because it's genuinely the whole idea.

Make it concrete. Write a `Student` class with three fields — `name`, `rollNo`, `cgpa` — and one method, `introduce()`, that prints them. Now build objects: `Student arun = new Student();` and `Student divya = new Student();`. Your class describes what *every* student has; each object is one actual classmate with their own values. Change Arun's CGPA and Divya's doesn't move — every object carries its own copy of the instance variables. Sixty students in your class, one attendance-register format: that's sixty objects, one class.

Why organise code this way at all? Because real programs juggle hundreds of related values. Without objects, a student's name lives in one array, their roll number in another, their CGPA in a third, and you pray the indexes stay lined up. With objects, everything about one student travels together, and the methods that act on that data live right next to it.

One habit to start on day one: make fields `private` and reach them through methods (getters and setters). It feels like pointless extra typing this week. It becomes obviously right the first time a bug corrupts a field and you can find every place that touches it in one search. This habit has a name — encapsulation — and it's one of the four OOP pillars interviewers will ask you to list.
tips: Class = design, object = one real thing built from it. If you can say it with a college example, the interview answer is done.
Every `new` creates a separate object with its own field values — two objects never share instance variables.
Start writing `private` fields + getters now; 'what is encapsulation?' is a guaranteed fresher question.
Learn to list the four OOP pillars — encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction — with one example each.

resources.title: #21 Class And Object Theory in Java

Declaring Classes — Oracle official tutorial

topics.title: Constructors, this, and static
topics.summary_md:
Three pieces of plumbing that make classes actually usable.

**Constructors** run automatically when you write `new`. Their job: make sure no object is born half-empty. Instead of creating a `Student` and then setting name, roll number, and CGPA in three separate lines (and forgetting one), write a constructor that demands them upfront: `new Student("Arun", 101, 8.2)`. A constructor has the same name as the class and **no return type** — writing `void` there turns it into an ordinary method, a mistake that compiles fine and fails silently.

**`this`** means "the current object". You need it mostly when a parameter and a field share a name: `this.name = name;` says "my field gets the parameter's value".

**`static`** flips the ownership: a static field or method belongs to the *class*, not to any object — one shared copy, total. Think of the difference between your ID card and the department notice board. Every student has their own ID card (instance field). There's exactly one notice board that everyone reads (static field). A `collegeName` field should be static — it's the same for all 60 objects; keeping 60 copies would be silly. A static counter incremented inside the constructor tells you how many objects ever got created — a classic exam question.

And now you can finally decode part of `public static void main`: main is static so the JVM can call it *before any object exists*. Nobody has created an object of your class when the program starts — so the starting point can't belong to one.
tips: A constructor with a return type isn't a constructor — it's a normal method that never runs. Java won't warn you.
Static methods can't touch instance fields directly — there's no 'current object' to read them from.
'Why is main static?' — because the JVM calls it before any object exists. Two lines, interview done.
If every object would store the same value, make it static; if each object needs its own, keep it instance.

topics.title: Inheritance and overriding: build on what exists
topics.summary_md:
Inheritance lets one class start from another instead of starting from zero. Write `class Professor extends CollegeMember` and `Professor` gets every field and method of `CollegeMember` for free, then adds its own.

Here's the working example for this topic. A `CollegeMember` class has `name`, `id`, and a `showId()` method. `Student extends CollegeMember` adds `cgpa`; `Professor extends CollegeMember` adds `department`. The shared stuff lives once, in the parent. Fix a bug in `showId()` and both children get the fix — that's the point.

The rule for when to use it: **is-a**. A Student *is a* CollegeMember — inherit. A Student *has a* timetable — that's a field, not a parent. Forcing inheritance where the is-a sentence sounds wrong creates the worst kind of tangled code, and "when would you NOT use inheritance?" is a real interview question.

**Overriding** is the child replacing an inherited method with its own version: `Student` can define its own `showId()` that also prints the CGPA. Always mark it `@Override` — the annotation makes the compiler verify you're genuinely overriding, so a typo like `showID()` becomes a compile error instead of a silently separate method.

Two more facts you'll be asked: `super.showId()` calls the parent's version from inside the child, and constructors are **not** inherited — but the child's constructor always runs the parent's first (via `super(...)`), so the parent's part of the object is built before the child's part.
tips: Use inheritance only when 'X is a Y' sounds natural out loud. 'Has a' means a field, not a parent class.
Always write `@Override` — it turns a method-name typo into a compile error instead of a silent bug.
Overriding vs overloading is the single most-asked fresher Java question. Prepare a two-line answer with one example each.
Child constructors always call the parent constructor first — `super(...)` must be the first line if you write it.

topics.title: Polymorphism, abstract classes, and interfaces
topics.summary_md:
This is the topic that separates "knows Java syntax" from "thinks in Java" — and it's where interviews spend the most time.

**Polymorphism** means one reference, many behaviours. Write `CollegeMember m = new Student();` — the reference type is the parent, the object is the child. Call `m.showId()` and *the Student's version runs*, because Java decides which overridden method to run by looking at the **object's actual type at runtime**, not the reference type. The reference type only decides which methods you're *allowed* to call. Say those two sentences in an interview and you've answered the most common OOP question cleanly.

**Abstract classes** are half-finished blueprints. `abstract class Shape` can declare `abstract double area();` — no body — forcing every child to fill it in. You can never write `new Shape()`; a "just a shape" object makes no sense, and the compiler agrees.

**Interfaces** go further: a pure contract, no state. Here's the example to build: a `Payment` interface with one method, `pay(int amount)`. Then `Upi`, `Card`, and `Cash` classes each implement it their own way — like the college canteen accepting GPay, card, or cash without caring which one you pull out. Code that works with `Payment` doesn't know or care which implementation shows up. Next semester someone adds `Wallet` and nothing else changes. That's "program to an interface", and it's the design idea underneath Spring, which you'll meet later in this pathway.

A class can implement many interfaces but extend only one class — that's usually the deciding factor between the two, and a guaranteed interview question.
tips: Parent reference, child object — the child's overridden method runs. This is *the* Java interview question; practice saying it aloud.
Abstract class vs interface: prepare three differences (state, constructors, multiple implementation) with one example.
You can't `new` an abstract class or an interface — but you can point a reference of that type at any implementation.
Remember `Payment`/`Upi`/`Card` — one example that answers polymorphism, abstraction, and interface questions all at once.

topics.title: Strings: immutable, pooled, and asked in every interview
topics.summary_md:
Strings look simple and hide two of the most-asked fresher interview questions. Both come from one fact: **a String can never be changed after creation**. Every "modification" — `concat`, `toUpperCase`, `+` — quietly builds a *new* String and leaves the original untouched.

Think of a laminated ID card. Name spelled wrong? Nobody edits the lamination — the office prints a fresh card and the old one goes in a drawer. `name.toUpperCase()` works the same way: you get a new card back, and if you don't catch it in a variable (`name = name.toUpperCase();`), you're still holding the old one. Half of all beginner String bugs are exactly this — calling a method and ignoring the returned value.

Immutability is also why `==` misleads. Java keeps a **String pool**: identical literals like `String a = "chai"; String b = "chai";` share one pooled object, so `a == b` happens to be true. But `new String("chai")` forces a separate object, and `==` turns false even though the text matches. Moral, one more time: **`.equals()` compares text; `==` compares whether it's literally the same object.** Interviewers set this trap deliberately.

Last piece: because every `+` makes a new String, building a big string inside a loop creates thousands of throwaway objects. Use **`StringBuilder`** there — it's a mutable workspace with an `append()` method, built for exactly this. "Difference between String and StringBuilder" is a standard question; now you know the answer is really "immutable vs mutable".
tips: `.equals()` for text comparison, always. `==` on Strings is the oldest trap in the Java interview book.
String methods return a new String — assign the result or nothing happens.
Building strings in a loop? StringBuilder. Mention it unprompted and you sound a year more experienced.
'Why are Strings immutable?' — security, safe sharing via the pool, and safe use as HashMap keys. Three words to remember.

topics.title: Stack, heap, and garbage collection — where your objects actually live
topics.summary_md:
You can write a lot of Java without knowing where objects live in memory. But the day you hit your first `NullPointerException` — and you will — this mental model is what turns panic into a two-minute fix.

Java splits memory into two areas. The **stack** holds method calls and local variables — small, fast, cleaned up automatically the moment a method returns. The **heap** holds every object you create with `new`. Your local variable doesn't contain the object; it contains a **reference** — the object's address on the heap.

Hostel version: the heap is the hostel building, and each object is an occupied room. Your variable is the room key in your pocket. `Student s1 = new Student();` allots a room and hands you the key. `Student s2 = s1;` doesn't build a second room — it cuts a duplicate key to the *same* room, which is why changing `s2.name` also changes what `s1` sees. And `s1 = null;` means you're holding a keyring with no key: ask it for a room (`s1.getName()`) and Java throws `NullPointerException` — read that as "you used a reference that points to nothing", find the line, and check what's null.

When a room has no keys left anywhere — no reference points to the object — the **garbage collector** reclaims it in the background. You don't free memory manually in Java; that's the JVM's job. You can't force GC either — `System.gc()` is only a polite request, a detail interviewers like to check.
tips: Objects live on the heap; local variables and references live on the stack. One line, frequently asked.
Copying a reference copies the key, not the room — two variables, one object.
`NullPointerException` = you called something on a reference that points to nothing. Find what's null; don't just catch it and hide it.
You can't force garbage collection — `System.gc()` is a suggestion the JVM may ignore.

modules.title: Collections & Generics
modules.description: ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet, and friends — the toolbox you'll reach for in every real Java program, plus the generics that keep it type-safe.

topics.title: ArrayList: the array that grows
topics.summary_md:
Arrays have one painful limitation: you must declare the size upfront, and it never changes. Real life doesn't work that way.

Picture maintaining the list of students who've submitted their assignment. Submissions trickle in all week — 3 today, 11 tomorrow, a panic burst before the deadline. With an array you'd have to guess a size (`new String[60]`?) and track how many slots are actually used. With `ArrayList<String> submitted = new ArrayList<>();` you just call `submitted.add(name)` every time someone submits, and the list grows by itself.

The API you'll use daily: `add(x)` appends, `get(i)` reads by position, `size()` counts, `remove(...)` deletes, `contains(x)` checks membership. Under the hood it's still an array — when it fills up, ArrayList quietly creates a bigger one and copies everything over. That's why reading by index stays fast, exactly like an array.

Two things trip up beginners. First, collections can't hold primitives — it's `ArrayList<Integer>`, never `ArrayList<int>`. Java auto-converts between `int` and `Integer` (autoboxing), so it mostly feels invisible. Second, that same fact hides a nasty trap: on an `ArrayList<Integer>`, `remove(2)` removes *the element at index 2*, while `remove(Integer.valueOf(2))` removes *the value 2*. When numbers vanish from the wrong place, remember this paragraph.

When to still use a plain array: fixed-size data (marks in exactly 5 subjects) and DSA problems where the size is given. Everywhere else in application code, ArrayList is the default.
tips: `ArrayList<int>` won't compile — type parameters need wrapper classes like `Integer`.
On a list of Integers, `remove(2)` removes by index, not by value. A genuinely evil trap — remember it exists.
'Array vs ArrayList' is a standard fresher question: fixed vs growable, primitives vs objects, length vs size().
Don't remove elements from a list while for-each looping over it — you'll meet ConcurrentModificationException.

resources.title: Introduction to Arrays and ArrayList in Java
ArrayList in Java: Demo & Methods
Guide to the Java ArrayList

topics.title: HashMap and HashSet: instant lookups
topics.summary_md:
Here's the problem these two solve. You have 60 students, and given a roll number you need the name. With a list you'd scan student by student until you find it. Fine for 60. Painful for 60,000. A **HashMap** answers in one step regardless of size.

A HashMap stores **key → value** pairs: `HashMap<Integer, String> students = new HashMap<>();` then `students.put(101, "Arun");` and later `students.get(101)` returns `"Arun"` instantly. It's the digital version of the front index page of your attendance register — you don't flip through pages; you look up the roll number directly. The magic word is *hashing*: the key's `hashCode()` tells the map exactly which bucket to check, so lookups take roughly the same time whether the map holds 10 entries or 10 lakh. That "roughly O(1)" behaviour is why maps show up in nearly every interview answer that starts with "how would you make this faster?"

Rules to internalise: keys are **unique** — `put(101, "Divya")` after `put(101, "Arun")` silently *replaces* Arun; it doesn't add a second entry. `get` on a missing key returns `null`, not an error — check with `containsKey` when it matters. And iteration order is **not** insertion order; a HashMap keeps things in whatever order hashing dictates.

**HashSet** is the same machinery with only keys, no values: a bag of unique things. `add()` a duplicate and the set silently ignores it — which makes "count unique visitors" a two-line program: add everything, print `size()`.
tips: put() with an existing key replaces the old value silently — no error, no warning.
get() on a missing key returns null; calling a method on that null is a classic NullPointerException source.
Need uniqueness or fast 'have I seen this?' checks → HashSet. Need to look values up by a key → HashMap. Say it that simply in interviews.
Never rely on a HashMap's iteration order. If order matters, that's LinkedHashMap or TreeMap — worth one line of reading.

A Guide to Java HashMap

topics.title: Generics: why the angle brackets exist
topics.summary_md:
You've been typing `ArrayList<String>` without asking what the angle brackets do. Time to ask.

Before generics, Java collections held plain `Object`s — anything could go in. A list meant for names would happily accept an `Integer` some teammate added by mistake, and you'd only find out at *runtime*, with a crash (`ClassCastException`), possibly weeks later on someone else's machine. Generics move that discovery to *compile time*: declare `ArrayList<String>` and the compiler rejects `list.add(42)` on the spot, before the program ever runs. A bug caught at compile time costs you ten seconds; the same bug at runtime can cost an evening.

Think of the hostel storeroom. One shelf has labelled boxes — "chargers", "books", "medicines" — and one has an unlabelled carton where everything got dumped. Finding your charger in the labelled box is instant, and nobody accidentally drops a slipper in there. The unlabelled carton is a raw, pre-generics collection: everything fits, and every retrieval is a gamble. `<String>` is the label.

Read `ArrayList<String>` aloud as "ArrayList *of* String" and the syntax stops looking alien. The second win: no more casting — `get(0)` hands you a `String` directly, not an `Object` you must cast and pray over.

Two facts for interviews: type parameters must be reference types (`Integer`, not `int`), and generics exist *only at compile time* — the JVM erases them (type erasure), so an `ArrayList<String>` and an `ArrayList<Integer>` are the same class at runtime. You don't need to master erasure now; knowing the word is enough.
tips: Generics buy compile-time safety, not speed — if an interviewer asks 'does it make code faster?', the answer is no.
Type parameters can't be primitives: `List<Integer>`, never `List<int>`.
The diamond `new ArrayList<>()` lets the compiler infer the type — write it on the right side, full type on the left.
Drop the phrase 'type erasure' when generics come up in interviews — then explain it in one line: generics vanish at runtime.

Why Use Generics? — Oracle official tutorial

topics.title: Sorting your way: Comparable and Comparator
topics.summary_md:
`Collections.sort(list)` sorts numbers and strings out of the box. But real programs sort *objects* — and Java has no idea whether two `Student` objects should be ordered by marks, name, or roll number. You have to tell it. Java gives you two ways.

**Comparable** bakes a natural order into the class itself. Your `Student` class implements `Comparable<Student>` and defines `compareTo(other)` — return a negative number if `this` comes first, positive if `other` does, zero for a tie. `Integer` and `String` already do this, which is why plain `sort` works on them.

**Comparator** keeps the ordering *outside* the class, so you can have many. This is the one you'll actually use daily, especially as a lambda: `list.sort((a, b) -> b.marks - a.marks)` sorts by marks descending.

Concrete task — build the department rank list: highest marks first, and when two students tie, alphabetical by name. One chained comparator does it: `list.sort(Comparator.comparingInt((Student s) -> s.marks).reversed().thenComparing(s -> s.name));`. Tie-breaking is exactly what written-test questions poke at, so write this once with your own class and run it.

The sign convention is everything. `(a, b) -> a.marks - b.marks` is ascending: a negative result means "a first", and small-minus-big is negative. Flip to `b.marks - a.marks` for descending. If a custom sort ever comes out backwards, you flipped the subtraction — everyone does it at least once.

One-line summary for interviews: Comparable is *the* default order, defined inside the class; Comparator is *an* order, defined outside, and you can have as many as you like.
tips: compareTo/compare contract: negative = first argument comes first, positive = second does, zero = tie. Everything else follows from this.
Sort came out backwards? You wrote `a - b` where you needed `b - a`. Check the subtraction before anything else.
Prefer `Comparator.comparing(...)` with `.reversed()` and `.thenComparing(...)` — it reads like English and avoids overflow bugs in subtraction.
'Comparable vs Comparator' is a top-five fresher question: inside vs outside the class, one order vs many.

resources.title: #95 Comparator vs Comparable in Java
Java Collections Framework | Java Placement Course
Comparator and Comparable in Java

topics.title: Queues, stacks, and when a list isn't the right shape
topics.summary_md:
Some data has a *direction*, and picking the structure that matches the direction makes code simpler and faster.

A **queue** is FIFO — first in, first out. The canteen line at 1 pm: whoever came first gets served first, new people join at the back. In Java: `Queue<String> line = new ArrayDeque<>();` with `offer()` to join, `poll()` to serve, `peek()` to see who's next without serving them. Anything processed "in arrival order" — print jobs, support tickets, BFS in graphs later — is a queue.

A **stack** is LIFO — last in, first out. The pile of steel plates at that same canteen counter: the plate you take is the last one washed and stacked. Java: `Deque<String> plates = new ArrayDeque<>();` with `push()` and `pop()`. Undo in an editor, the Back button, matching brackets in a compiler, and method calls themselves (remember the call *stack*?) — all stacks.

Note both used `ArrayDeque`. Java has a legacy `Stack` class, but it's an old design mistake (it extends Vector, carries lock overhead, and lets you break LIFO by inserting in the middle) — the official recommendation is `ArrayDeque` for both stacks and queues. Saying that in an interview is a small, genuine flex.

What about **LinkedList**? It implements both List and Deque, and textbooks praise its O(1) insert-at-ends. In practice ArrayList and ArrayDeque beat it almost every time because of how CPUs read memory. Know what it is, be ready to compare it with ArrayList in an interview — and then rarely use it.
tips: FIFO = queue = canteen line. LIFO = stack = plate pile. Two images, permanent memory.
Use ArrayDeque for stacks and queues — the legacy Stack class is officially discouraged; saying why earns interview points.
poll() and peek() return null on an empty queue; pop() on an empty deque throws. Check before you take.
'ArrayList vs LinkedList' is a common question — know the textbook answer (index reads vs end inserts) and the honest one (ArrayList usually wins).

resources.title: Stacks and Queues Complete Tutorial - Theory + Implementation + Types (Dynamic, Circular)
Stack Data Structure Implementation | Java Course in Tamil | Logic First Tamil
Guide to the Java Queue Interface

topics.title: equals() and hashCode(): the contract behind every HashMap
topics.summary_md:
This is the deep-dive that turns "I use HashMap" into "I understand HashMap" — and it's a favourite question for freshers who list Java on their resume.

Start with the puzzle. You write a `Student` class, create two objects with the same roll number — `new Student(101)` twice — and add both to a `HashSet`. The set's size is **2**. But roll number 101 is one person! Why the duplicate? Because by default, Java's `equals()` compares *references* — "are these literally the same object in memory?" — and your two Students are two separate objects, like two printed copies of the same ID card. If your program should treat same-roll-number as same-student, *you* must say so: override `equals()` to compare roll numbers.

But overriding `equals()` alone isn't enough, and here's the part interviews probe. Hash-based collections find things in two steps: `hashCode()` picks the bucket, then `equals()` confirms the match within it. If two "equal" Students return different hash codes, they land in different buckets, and the HashSet never even compares them — duplicates sneak in anyway, and `map.get(student)` returns null for an entry you *know* you put in. Hence **the contract: if two objects are equal, they must have the same hashCode.** Override both or neither.

In practice you don't hand-write these: your IDE generates them (or a `record` gives them free — worth a look). What matters is understanding *why* both exist. Run the broken version once — two students, same roll number, HashSet of size 2 — then fix it and watch the size drop to 1. That five-minute experiment is the whole topic.
tips: The contract, one line: equal objects must return equal hash codes. Override both methods or neither.
Symptoms of breaking it: duplicates in a HashSet, map.get() returning null for a key you definitely put in.
Never mutate the fields used in hashCode() after putting an object in a HashSet or as a map key — the object gets lost inside.
Mention that Java records generate equals/hashCode automatically — a modern touch interviewers notice.

The Contracts of equals() and hashCode()

modules.title: Exceptions & File I/O
modules.description: How Java handles things going wrong — and how your programs read and write real files. Both show up in every fresher interview and every real codebase.

topics.title: Exceptions: when your program hits a wall
topics.summary_md:
An exception is Java's way of saying "something went wrong here, and I can't continue". Instead of returning a weird value like `-1` and hoping you check it, Java stops, builds an object describing the problem, and throws it up through your code until something catches it — or the program dies with a stack trace.

Here's a concrete example. You write a small app that asks for a roll number and someone types `twelve` instead of `12`. `Integer.parseInt("twelve")` can't produce a number, so it throws a `NumberFormatException`. Without handling, your app crashes. With a `try`/`catch`, you catch it, print "That's not a number — try again", and keep running. Same input, completely different experience.

The pattern is simple: put risky code inside `try`, put your response to failure inside `catch`. The exception object tells you what happened (`getMessage()`) and where (the stack trace — read it top to bottom, the first line naming *your* file is usually the culprit).

Don't fall into the fresher trap of wrapping everything in `try { ... } catch (Exception e) {}` with an empty catch block. That doesn't handle the problem — it hides it, and you'll spend hours debugging silence. Catch what you can actually respond to, and let the rest surface loudly.
tips: An empty catch block is worse than a crash — at least a crash tells you where it hurts.
Interviewers love 'what's the difference between an exception and an error?' — Error means the JVM itself is in trouble (like OutOfMemoryError); you don't catch those.
Learn to read a stack trace top-down; the first line mentioning your own class is where to look.

resources.title: #76 What is Exception in Java
Java Tutorials For Beginners In Hindi
Exceptions — dev.java official tutorial

topics.title: Checked vs unchecked: what the compiler forces on you
topics.summary_md:
Java splits exceptions into two families, and interviewers ask about this constantly.

**Checked exceptions** are problems the compiler makes you plan for. Try to open a file and Java won't even compile your code until you either catch `IOException` or add `throws IOException` to your method. The logic: a missing file isn't a bug in your code — it's the outside world misbehaving — so you must decide upfront what to do about it.

**Unchecked exceptions** (subclasses of `RuntimeException`) are usually *your* bugs: `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException`. The compiler stays quiet because the real fix isn't a catch block — it's correcting your code.

A concrete example: your program reads today's timetable from `timetable.txt`. The file might genuinely not exist on a friend's laptop — that's `FileNotFoundException`, checked, and the compiler forces you to handle it. But if you then call `.length()` on a line you never assigned, that's a `NullPointerException`, unchecked — no catch block fixes forgetting to assign a variable.

The `throws` keyword is just passing the decision upward: "I won't handle this here; whoever calls me must." That's fine for small helper methods, but *somewhere* in your program, someone has to actually deal with it.
tips: Quick interview answer: checked = compiler forces handling, usually outside-world problems; unchecked = RuntimeException family, usually your own bugs.
Don't 'fix' a NullPointerException by catching it — find out why the value was null.
throws on main() is fine for practice programs, lazy in real ones — know the difference and say so in interviews.

resources.title: #79 Exception Hierarchy in Java
Exception Handling in Java in Hindi | Java Programming | Java Tutorial | Great Learning
Unchecked Exceptions — The Controversy (The Java Tutorials)
Checked and Unchecked Exceptions in Java

topics.title: finally and try-with-resources: always clean up
topics.summary_md:
Files, database connections, network sockets — these are borrowed things. If your code opens them and crashes before closing them, they stay stuck open. Do that in a loop and your program eventually can't open anything at all.

Think of the computer lab at college: you log in, and whether your program runs perfectly or crashes halfway, you're supposed to log out before leaving. The lab doesn't care *why* you're leaving — the machine must be freed either way. That's exactly what cleanup code is.

The old tool for this is `finally`: a block that runs whether the `try` succeeded, threw, or even returned early. It works, but closing a file inside `finally` is clumsy — `close()` itself can throw, so you end up with a try inside a finally. Ugly.

Java 7 fixed this with **try-with-resources**: declare the resource inside the parentheses — `try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("notes.txt")))` — and Java guarantees it gets closed, automatically, in the reverse order you opened things. No `finally` needed for cleanup. Anything implementing `AutoCloseable` works this way, which covers files, JDBC connections, and most things you'll ever open.

Use try-with-resources every single time you open something. It's less code *and* more correct — a rare combination — and it's the version interviewers expect from you in 2026.
tips: finally runs even after a return statement inside try — a favourite tricky interview question.
If you write .close() by hand in 2026, expect the interviewer to ask why you didn't use try-with-resources.
Multiple resources in one try(...) close in reverse order of opening — worth saying out loud in interviews.

resources.title: The try-with-resources Statement (The Java Tutorials)
#84 try with resources in Java
#44 Exception Handling in Java | In Tamil | Java Tutorial Series | Error Makes Clever

topics.title: Reading and writing files, the sane way
topics.summary_md:
Programs that forget everything when they close aren't very useful. Files are the simplest way to make data survive — and file I/O questions show up in almost every fresher-level Java round.

Java gives you several tools, so here's the map. For reading text line by line, wrap a `FileReader` in a `BufferedReader` and loop with `readLine()` until it returns `null`. The buffering matters: `FileReader` alone fetches one character at a time from disk, while `BufferedReader` grabs big chunks and hands them to you from memory — dramatically faster for anything beyond a few lines. For writing, `FileWriter` (again, wrapped in a `BufferedWriter`) does the job; pass `true` as the second argument to append instead of overwrite.

The modern shortcut is the `Files` class from NIO: `Files.readAllLines(Path.of("students.txt"))` gives you a `List<String>` in one line, and `Files.writeString(...)` writes in one line. Great for small files; avoid it for huge ones since it loads everything into memory.

A concrete example: build a tiny attendance tracker. Each class day, append a line like `2026-07-04,42 present` to `attendance.csv`. Later, read all lines, split each on the comma, and total up the numbers. That one exercise teaches you reading, writing, appending, and parsing — do it once and file questions stop being scary.

Always open files with try-with-resources. Always.
tips: readLine() returns null at end of file — checking for empty string "" instead is a classic beginner bug.
FileWriter overwrites by default; new FileWriter(file, true) appends. Freshers lose data to this one constantly.
Files.readAllLines is one line of code but loads the whole file into memory — say that trade-off in interviews.
Prefer relative paths in practice code; hard-coded paths like C:\Users\me\... break on every other machine.

Reading a File in Java

topics.title: Writing your own exceptions (and when not to)
topics.summary_md:
Sometimes Java's built-in exceptions don't say what actually went wrong in *your* app. `IllegalArgumentException` is technically correct everywhere, which means it's precise nowhere.

A concrete example: you're building a canteen wallet app for your hostel. When someone with ₹20 tries to buy a ₹35 meal, throwing a plain `RuntimeException("error")` tells the caller nothing. Throwing your own `InsufficientBalanceException` — carrying the shortfall amount — lets the calling code catch exactly that case and show "Add ₹15 to continue", while letting genuinely unexpected problems crash loudly like they should.

Writing one is short: extend `Exception` (checked — caller must plan for it) or `RuntimeException` (unchecked), and add a constructor that passes a clear message to `super(...)`. Add fields if the catcher needs data, like that shortfall amount. That's the whole recipe.

Two pieces of senior advice. First, don't create an exception zoo — five well-named exceptions beat thirty nobody remembers. If a built-in like `IllegalArgumentException` already says it clearly, use it. Second, when you catch one exception and throw another, pass the original as the *cause* (`super(message, cause)`), so the stack trace still shows where the trouble started. Losing the cause is like tearing the first page off a complaint letter — the rest stops making sense.

This is a deep-dive topic: skippable on a tight schedule, but it makes your projects look noticeably more professional in interviews.
tips: Name exceptions after the problem, not the place: InsufficientBalanceException, not WalletException.
Always chain the cause when re-throwing — a stack trace without the root cause wastes hours.
Interview angle: 'when would you make a custom exception checked vs unchecked?' — checked if the caller can realistically recover.
