modules.title: JDBC & MySQL
modules.description: Connect your Java code to a real database. This is where your programs stop forgetting everything — and where service-company interviews spend a lot of their time.

topics.title: MySQL setup + just enough SQL
topics.summary_md:
Before Java can talk to a database, you need a database — and enough SQL to hold a conversation with it.

Install MySQL Community Server (free) and use the command-line client or MySQL Workbench. Then learn the core moves. `CREATE DATABASE college;` makes a database. Inside it, a table is like one sheet of a register: `CREATE TABLE students (roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50), marks INT);` defines the columns once, and every row follows that shape.

Four verbs cover 90% of fresher-level SQL. `INSERT` adds a row. `SELECT` reads rows — `SELECT name FROM students WHERE marks >= 75;` pulls only what you asked for. `UPDATE ... WHERE` changes rows. `DELETE ... WHERE` removes them. The `WHERE` clause is the difference between changing one student's marks and changing *everyone's* — run `SELECT` with your `WHERE` first to see what you're about to touch.

A concrete example to practice with: build a `students` table for your own class. Insert ten classmates with roll numbers and marks, then answer real questions with queries — who scored above 80? What's the class average (`AVG(marks)`)? Sort by marks descending. Twenty minutes of this and SQL stops feeling foreign.

Also learn what a **primary key** is (a column that uniquely identifies each row — roll number, not name, because two students can share a name). Interviewers ask this early and often.
tips: UPDATE or DELETE without a WHERE clause touches every row in the table — check twice, run once.
Primary key vs unique key vs foreign key — service companies ask this trio in almost every interview.
Practice writing queries by hand before touching Java; debugging SQL inside Java strings is much harder.

resources.title: Getting Started with MySQL
SQL Tutorial for Beginners (Complete Course using MySQL)
SQL Basic for Beginners Tutorial in Tamil | Learn SQL With MySQL | sql tutorial for beginners
Mysql Tutorial

topics.title: JDBC: how Java dials the database
topics.summary_md:
JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is Java's standard way of talking to any database. Your code speaks JDBC; a **driver** translates that into whatever the specific database understands. Swap MySQL for Postgres later and your Java code barely changes — only the driver and URL do.

For MySQL, the driver is a library called **Connector/J**. You add it to your project (a jar on the classpath for now — Maven makes this one line in the next module), and then connect:

```java
Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
    "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/college", "root", "yourpassword");
```

Read that URL like a postal address: `jdbc:mysql` is the protocol (which driver to use), `localhost` is the machine the database lives on (your own laptop), `3306` is MySQL's default port, and `college` is the database name. Wrong password or MySQL not running? You get a `SQLException` — a checked exception, so the compiler forces you to handle it. Now the previous module pays off.

A concrete example: the first program to write is a five-line "can I connect?" check — open the connection inside try-with-resources, print "connected!", done. When it works, break it on purpose: stop the MySQL service, retype the password wrongly, misspell the database name. Read each error message carefully. Interviewers can tell who has actually seen these errors and who has only watched videos.

A `Connection` is a live phone call to the database — always open it with try-with-resources so it hangs up properly.
tips: You do NOT need Class.forName(...) with modern drivers — mention that in interviews to sound current.
'Communications link failure' almost always means MySQL isn't running or the port is wrong — check the service first.
Never hard-code the real password in code you push to GitHub; recruiters do look at repos.

resources.title: Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) by Navin Reddy
Establishing a Connection — JDBC Basics (The Java Tutorials)
Java JDBC Tutorial for Beginners in Hindi and English

topics.title: CRUD from Java: Statement and ResultSet
topics.summary_md:
Once connected, you send SQL from Java and read what comes back. Two methods carry all the weight, and mixing them up is the classic fresher slip.

`executeQuery(sql)` is for `SELECT`. It returns a `ResultSet` — think of it as a cursor sitting *above* the first row of results. Each call to `rs.next()` moves it down one row and returns `true` if a row is there, so the standard loop is `while (rs.next()) { ... }`. Inside, pull values by column name: `rs.getInt("marks")`, `rs.getString("name")`. Forgetting that the cursor starts *before* the first row — and calling `rs.getString(...)` without calling `next()` first — is the number one JDBC runtime error.

`executeUpdate(sql)` is for `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, and `DELETE`. No rows come back; instead it returns an `int` — how many rows were affected. That number is useful: after an `UPDATE`, `0` quietly tells you your `WHERE` clause matched nothing.

A concrete example: extend the students table from earlier. Write one Java program with four small methods — `addStudent`, `listToppers` (marks ≥ 75), `updateMarks`, `removeStudent` — and a tiny menu in `main` to call them. That single program is your CRUD muscle memory, and a version of it is asked in service-company machine rounds all the time.

`Connection`, `Statement`, and `ResultSet` are all resources — stack them in one try-with-resources and they close cleanly in reverse order.
tips: executeQuery for SELECT, executeUpdate for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE — interviewers check this in the first five minutes.
rs.next() must be called before reading the first row — the cursor starts before row one.
Check the int from executeUpdate; 0 rows affected usually means your WHERE clause missed.

resources.title: Processing SQL Statements with JDBC (The Java Tutorials)
Java JDBC Tutorial for Beginners in Hindi and English
Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) by Navin Reddy
Introduction to JDBC

topics.title: PreparedStatement: the SQL injection vaccine
topics.summary_md:
Here's the scariest demo in beginner Java. Say your login code builds SQL by gluing strings:

```java
String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + input + "'";
```

A concrete example of it going wrong: a prankster in your class types `' OR '1'='1` into the username box of your mini-project. Your string-glue produces `WHERE name = '' OR '1'='1'` — a condition that's true for *every* row. They just logged in as everyone. That attack is called **SQL injection**, and string concatenation is the open door it walks through.

`PreparedStatement` closes the door. You write the SQL once with `?` placeholders, then fill them in:

```java
PreparedStatement ps = con.prepareStatement(
    "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?");
ps.setString(1, input);
```

The key idea: the database receives the *query shape* and the *user's text* separately. Whatever the prankster types is treated as plain data — a weird username that matches nobody — never as SQL. The `?` values are numbered from 1, not 0, which trips up every array-brained Java learner once.

There are two bonuses: the database can compile the query once and reuse the plan when you run it many times with different values, and you stop fighting quote-escaping bugs when names like `D'Souza` show up.

The rule to tattoo on your brain: **if a value comes from a user, it goes in through a `?`.** No exceptions, no "just this once".
tips: PreparedStatement parameters start at index 1, not 0 — everyone hits this once.
'How would you prevent SQL injection?' is a guaranteed interview question — PreparedStatement is the expected answer.
Even if you validate input elsewhere, still use placeholders — defence shouldn't depend on one gate.

resources.title: Using Prepared Statements (The Java Tutorials)
Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) by Navin Reddy
SQL Injection 💉
SQL Injection and How to Prevent It?

topics.title: Transactions and pooling: the all-or-nothing rules
topics.summary_md:
Some operations only make sense together. A concrete example: your department fest's ticket system does two updates when someone books — subtract one seat from `events`, and insert a row into `payments`. Now imagine the app crashes *between* the two. A seat vanished but no payment exists. Money and data no longer agree, and no single line of code was wrong.

**Transactions** fix this. By default JDBC runs in auto-commit mode — every statement is saved permanently the moment it runs. Turn that off with `con.setAutoCommit(false)`, run both updates, then call `con.commit()` to make them permanent *together*. If anything throws in between, call `con.rollback()` in your catch block and the database snaps back to how it was — as if the booking never started. All or nothing. That's the A in **ACID** (atomicity), a term worth knowing because interviewers love expanding the acronym.

The second idea here is **connection pooling**. Opening a database connection is genuinely slow — network handshake, authentication, session setup. Real applications don't open a fresh one per request; they keep a small set of ready connections (a *pool*) and lend them out, the way your hostel mess keeps plates washed and stacked instead of buying a new plate per meal. In plain JDBC projects you won't need a pool, but know the name **HikariCP** — it's the pool Spring Boot uses under the hood, and mentioning *why* pools exist is an easy way to stand out in interviews.

This is a deep-dive topic — skippable on a tight schedule, essential before Spring Boot.
tips: rollback() belongs in the catch block, and commit() is the last line of the try — get the shape right once and reuse it.
Interview one-liner: 'auto-commit treats every statement as its own transaction.'
Know ACID as more than letters — atomicity is the one transactions demos actually show.

resources.title: Using Transactions — JDBC Basics (The Java Tutorials)
Lec-88: ACID Properties of a Transaction | Database Management System
Introduction to HikariCP

modules.title: Maven & Build Basics
modules.description: Stop downloading jars by hand. Maven fetches your libraries, builds your project the same way on every machine, and is the doorway to Spring Boot.

topics.title: Why build tools exist (the pain Maven removes)
topics.summary_md:
You can survive college assignments with `javac Main.java` and `java Main`. Then projects grow, and the cracks show.

A concrete example: you and a friend split a mini-project — a student portal using MySQL. Your half works. You zip the code and send it over, and on their laptop it explodes with `ClassNotFoundException` — because the MySQL Connector jar is sitting in *your* downloads folder, added through *your* IDE settings, on *your* machine. They download a jar too, but a different version, and now you're debugging errors that aren't in anyone's code. Every hour spent on this is an hour not spent on the actual project.

Maven removes exactly this pain. You describe your project in one file — `pom.xml` — including which libraries you need and which versions. Anyone (or any server) with Maven can then run one command and get an identical build: dependencies downloaded, code compiled, tests run, jar produced. "Works on my machine" becomes "works on every machine".

Maven's other big idea is **convention over configuration**: instead of telling the tool where everything lives, you follow its standard folder layout — code in `src/main/java`, tests in `src/test/java` — and it just knows. Every Maven project on Earth looks the same inside, which is why you can open a stranger's repo and find your way around in seconds.

Spring Boot, which you'll meet soon, assumes Maven (or its cousin Gradle) from the first minute. Learn it now and that module gets much easier.
tips: If you've ever emailed a jar file to a teammate, you already understand why Maven exists — use that story in interviews.
Maven vs Gradle is a common interview aside: same job, Gradle uses code instead of XML — knowing both names is enough at fresher level.
The mvn command not being found usually means PATH isn't set — fix your environment once, properly.

resources.title: Introduction to Maven | Theory
Maven Crash Course | Learn maven in one video in Hindi
Maven in 5 Minutes

topics.title: pom.xml and dependencies: one file to rule the jars
topics.summary_md:
The `pom.xml` (Project Object Model) is your project's identity card plus shopping list. Two parts matter at fresher level.

First, **coordinates**. Every Maven project — yours included — is identified by three values: `groupId` (who made it, usually a reversed domain like `com.fledge`), `artifactId` (the project's name), and `version`. Together they're called GAV, and they name a library as precisely as a full postal address names a house.

Second, **dependencies**. Need MySQL Connector? Instead of hunting a download page, you add its coordinates inside `<dependencies>` in your pom. On the next build, Maven fetches it from **Maven Central** — a giant public warehouse of Java libraries — and caches it in a folder on your machine (`~/.m2/repository`), so it's downloaded once, not on every build.

A concrete example: last module you needed Connector/J for JDBC and had to fiddle with classpaths. With Maven that whole struggle becomes five lines of XML — `groupId` `com.mysql`, `artifactId` `mysql-connector-j`, and a version. Rebuild, and JDBC imports just resolve. That before/after is the whole sales pitch.

The quiet superpower is **transitive dependencies**: if the library you add needs three other libraries, Maven fetches those too, and the right versions of them. That's the tedious, error-prone work you were doing by hand without knowing it had a name.

When you need coordinates, search the library name on search.maven.org and copy the XML block. Everyone does it this way — seniors included.
tips: GAV = groupId, artifactId, version — if you can explain these three, most fresher Maven questions are done.
Deleting ~/.m2/repository fixes some 'corrupted jar' errors — but expect a long re-download, so it's a last resort.
Dependency version conflicts (two libraries wanting different versions of a third) are a classic interview follow-up — knowing the problem exists is enough at this stage.

resources.title: Introduction to the Dependency Mechanism
Maven Tutorial - Crash Course
Maven Crash Course | Learn maven in one video in Hindi
Apache Maven Tutorial

topics.title: The build lifecycle: what mvn package actually does
topics.summary_md:
Maven builds run through a fixed sequence of **phases**, like stations on a single train line: `validate → compile → test → package → install → deploy`. The rule that explains everything: asking for any phase runs *every phase before it* too. `mvn package` doesn't just zip your classes into a jar — it compiles first, then runs your tests, and only packages if they pass.

That ordering is a feature, not a delay. A concrete example: it's 11:50 pm, your team's mini-project demo is at 9 am, and your teammate pushes one "tiny" last-minute fix. They run `mvn package` to build the demo jar — and the build fails, because their fix broke a test. Annoying at midnight, but the broken version never made it into the jar you'd have carried to the demo. The lifecycle physically blocks packaging broken code.

The commands you'll actually type daily: `mvn compile` (quick "does it build?"), `mvn test` (run all tests), `mvn package` (produce the jar in the `target/` folder), and `mvn clean` (delete `target/` and start fresh — the combo `mvn clean package` is the standard "full rebuild"). `mvn install` goes one step further and copies your jar into your local `~/.m2` repository so *other* projects on your machine can depend on it.

One folder to understand: `target/` is where all generated stuff lands — compiled classes, test reports, the final jar. It's disposable by definition, which is why it belongs in `.gitignore`, never in your repo.
tips: Phases run in order — 'mvn package also runs tests' is a favourite interview check.
Never commit the target/ folder; it's generated output, and recruiters notice a messy repo.
Skipping tests with -DskipTests is for emergencies, not habit — be ready to defend it if an interviewer sees it in your README.

resources.title: Introduction to the Build Lifecycle
Maven Tutorial Practical
Maven Crash Course | Learn maven in one video in Hindi

topics.title: Plugins and the runnable jar (demo-day insurance)
topics.summary_md:
Here's a Maven secret: the lifecycle phases don't do any work themselves. Every phase just triggers **plugins** — `compile` runs the compiler plugin, `package` runs the jar plugin. Maven is a manager; plugins are the workers. Once you see that, the pom stops looking like magic.

The first plugin problem every beginner meets: you run `mvn package`, get your jar from `target/`, run `java -jar myapp.jar` — and hit `no main manifest attribute`. Two things are missing. The jar doesn't know which class holds `main()`, and it doesn't contain your dependencies — a plain jar has *your* classes only, so anything using MySQL Connector dies with `ClassNotFoundException` the moment it leaves your machine.

The fix is a **fat jar** (also called an uber jar): one file containing your classes *and* every dependency, with the main class recorded in its manifest. The **Maven Shade plugin** builds it — a copy-paste block in your pom with your main class's name filled in.

A concrete example of why this matters: project demo day, and the lab machine has Java but no IDE, no Maven, no wifi worth trusting. If you brought a fat jar on a pen drive, your demo is one command: `java -jar studentportal.jar`. If you brought source code, you're that team installing dependencies while the external examiner checks the time.

This is a deep-dive topic — skip it on a tight schedule. But when Spring Boot later hands you a runnable fat jar out of the box, you'll know exactly what problem it quietly solved.
tips: 'no main manifest attribute' means the jar doesn't know your main class — a manifest/plugin fix, not a code fix.
Fat jar vs plain jar is a neat interview differentiator at fresher level — most freshers can't explain it.
Test your jar with java -jar on a different machine (or at least a different folder) before demo day.

resources.title: Creating an Executable JAR with Maven
Introduction to Maven Plugins
Maven Tutorial Practical
Maven Crash Course | Learn maven in one video in Hindi

modules.title: Spring Boot Fundamentals
modules.description: The framework behind most Java backend jobs in India. You'll get a real app running in minutes, then learn what Spring is actually doing for you underneath.

topics.title: Why Spring Boot, and your first running app
topics.summary_md:
Spring is the framework most Java backend jobs in India run on. At its heart it does one job: it creates your objects, connects them, and handles the boring plumbing — so you write business logic, not setup code. Plain Spring, though, needs a lot of configuration before anything runs. Spring Boot fixes that. It picks sensible defaults for you (auto-configuration), bundles a web server inside your app (embedded Tomcat), and gives you *starters* — ready-made dependency bundles like `spring-boot-starter-web`.

Here's the fastest way to feel it. Go to **start.spring.io** and fill it like a fest registration form — project name, Java version, tick the "Spring Web" dependency — then click Generate. Unzip, open it in IntelliJ or VS Code, and run the main class. In under a minute you'll see the Spring banner in the console and a live server at `http://localhost:8080`. That's a production-grade Java web server, started from one class with one annotation.

Don't try to understand every file yet. Know these three: `pom.xml` lists your dependencies, the class with `@SpringBootApplication` is the entry point, and `application.properties` is where settings live. Everything else in this module grows out of this skeleton, so keep the project around — you'll turn it into a real API in the next module.
tips: Interviewers ask 'Spring vs Spring Boot' constantly — answer with the three gifts: auto-configuration, embedded server, starters.
If the app dies at startup with 'port 8080 already in use', set server.port in application.properties instead of restarting your laptop.
Match the Java version in pom.xml to the one installed on your machine — the mismatch is the most common first-run error.

resources.title: Spring Boot Tutorials | Full Course
Introduction to Spring Boot | What is Spring Boot | Spring Boot Tutorial in Hindi
Building an Application with Spring Boot — official guide

topics.title: IoC and dependency injection, minus the jargon
topics.summary_md:
Inversion of Control sounds scary; the idea is small. Normally your class creates what it needs: `StudentService` writes `new StudentRepository()`. With IoC you flip it — the class only *declares* what it needs, and the Spring container creates that object and hands it over. The handing-over part is dependency injection (DI).

Think of your college canteen. You don't walk into the kitchen and cook your own lunch. You say "one meals" at the counter, and the kitchen decides how it's made and hands you the plate. Your class is you at the counter; the Spring container is the kitchen. Your class never worries about how its dependencies get built.

Why do interviewers care so much? Two reasons. **Loose coupling**: if `StudentService` never says `new`, you can swap the real repository for a fake one in tests without touching the service. **One place for wiring**: object creation lives with the container, not scattered across your codebase.

In code, DI mostly looks like this: mark the class `@Service` or `@Component`, take dependencies as constructor parameters, and Spring fills them in. Prefer constructor injection over `@Autowired` on fields — dependencies stay visible, fields can be `final`, and it's what modern Spring codebases expect. When Spring can't inject something, read the error slowly: "required a bean of type … that could not be found" tells you exactly which piece is missing.
tips: 'Field injection vs constructor injection' is a favourite fresher question — say constructor, then say why: final fields, visible dependencies, easier tests.
IoC is the idea, DI is the technique. One sentence each is enough in an interview.
If you write `new` for a class that Spring manages, injection inside that object silently stops working — a classic beginner trap.

resources.title: #6 Dependency Injection using Spring Boot
Spring Boot Tutorial in Hindi
Intro to Inversion of Control and Dependency Injection with Spring

topics.title: @SpringBootApplication and friends
topics.summary_md:
Annotations are how you talk to Spring. You don't call the framework; you leave labels on your classes, and the framework acts on them at startup. A handful of labels cover 90% of what freshers write, so learn these well instead of memorising fifty.

`@SpringBootApplication` is three annotations wearing one coat: `@Configuration` (this class can define beans), `@EnableAutoConfiguration` (set things up based on what's on the classpath), and `@ComponentScan` (search this package and below for components).

Component scanning is worth picturing properly. Think of a hostel warden doing the night register: she walks every floor *of her own hostel* and notes who's in which room. Spring does the same walk through your base package, registering every class marked `@Component`, `@Service`, `@Repository`, or `@Controller` as a bean. And just like the warden never checks the hostel next door, Spring never scans packages *outside* your base package — put a class there and it simply doesn't exist to the container.

The stereotypes — `@Service` for business logic, `@Repository` for data access, `@Controller`/`@RestController` for web — all behave like `@Component` underneath. The names are for humans reading your code and for a few framework extras. Finally, `@Bean` on a method inside a `@Configuration` class registers whatever the method returns — your escape hatch for objects from libraries you can't annotate yourself.
tips: 'What does @SpringBootApplication contain?' is a standard fresher screen — name all three parts.
A 'bean not found' error usually means the class sits outside the scanned package. Check the package before checking anything else.
@Service vs @Component does the same thing technically — say 'it communicates intent' and you sound like you've read real code.

resources.title: Spring Boot Annotations Explained with Examples
Spring Boot Annotations in Hindi - @Component, @Service, @Autowired
Spring Boot Annotations — a practical tour

topics.title: application.properties and profiles
topics.summary_md:
Hard-coding values into Java is how beginners get stuck: a database password inside a class, a port number buried in code. Spring Boot's answer is `application.properties` — one text file where configuration lives, read at startup. Change the file, restart, done. No recompiling, no hunting through classes.

The entries are plain `key=value` lines: `server.port=9090`, `spring.datasource.url=...`, or your own keys like `fledge.exam-mode=true`, which you pull into code with `@Value("${fledge.exam-mode}")`.

Now the part that makes you sound experienced: **profiles**. Your app runs in different environments — your laptop while developing, a server in production — and each needs different settings. Think of university exams: you're the same student with the same preparation, but each exam puts you in a different hall with a different seat. Same code, different configuration per environment. You create `application-dev.properties` and `application-prod.properties`, then pick one by setting `spring.profiles.active=dev`. Values in the active profile file override the same keys in the base `application.properties` — base file for common settings, profile files for what differs.

Two habits to build now. Never commit real passwords — use environment variable placeholders like `${DB_PASSWORD}`. And when a setting mysteriously "doesn't apply", check which profile is active first; nine times out of ten the value is being overridden by another file.
tips: Property override order confuses everyone once — remember: active profile file beats application.properties.
Never push credentials to GitHub; recruiters do look at your repos. Use ${ENV_VAR} placeholders.
Interviewers like 'how do you run different configs for dev and prod?' — the word they want is profiles.

resources.title: application.properties and Profiles in Spring Boot
Spring Boot application.properties in Hindi
Properties with Spring and Spring Boot

topics.title: Bean scopes and lifecycle (deep-dive)
topics.summary_md:
By default, Spring creates exactly **one** instance of each bean and shares it everywhere it's injected. That's the *singleton* scope, and it surprises people. Picture the canteen's billing machine: one machine, every student's bill passes through it. Your `StudentService` is that machine — every request in your app uses the same object. This is efficient, but it comes with a rule: don't store per-user data in a singleton's fields, or two users' data will mix. That bug is real, ugly, and a favourite interview probe.

When you genuinely need a fresh object each time, ask for *prototype* scope with `@Scope("prototype")` — a new instance per injection or lookup. Web apps add `request` and `session` scopes, but as a fresher you'll rarely touch them; knowing they exist is enough.

Beans also have a lifecycle you can hook into. `@PostConstruct` marks a method to run right after Spring finishes building and injecting the bean — the right place for setup that needs dependencies ready, like loading a cache. `@PreDestroy` runs before the container shuts the bean down — the place to close connections or flush files.

Why is this a deep-dive and not core? Because you can build your first three projects without ever writing `@Scope`. But the singleton-statefulness trap and the `@PostConstruct` question show up in interviews for product companies, so return to this before those rounds.
tips: The trap question: 'is a Spring singleton thread-safe?' No — Spring gives one instance, thread safety is your job. Keep singletons stateless.
Constructor runs before injection completes; @PostConstruct runs after. Setup that touches dependencies goes in @PostConstruct.
Don't say 'I'd make everything prototype to be safe' — it signals you haven't understood why singleton is the default.

resources.title: Bean Scopes in Spring - Singleton vs Prototype
Spring Bean Life Cycle and Scopes in Hindi
Quick Guide to Spring Bean Scopes

topics.title: Actuator: your app's dashboard (deep-dive)
topics.summary_md:
Once your app is running on a server instead of your laptop, a new question appears: *is it healthy right now?* You can't attach a debugger to production. Spring Boot Actuator answers this by adding ready-made HTTP endpoints that report on your app from the inside.

Think of a bike's dashboard before a long trip: fuel gauge, engine warning light, speedometer. You don't open the engine to check these — the dashboard reports them while you ride. Actuator is that dashboard for your Spring Boot app. Add the `spring-boot-starter-actuator` dependency, restart, and `GET /actuator/health` answers `{"status":"UP"}`. That one endpoint is what load balancers and deployment platforms poll to decide whether your app should receive traffic.

There's more behind it: `/actuator/metrics` exposes memory, CPU, and request counts; `/actuator/info` shares build details; `/actuator/env` shows resolved configuration. Most endpoints are switched off over HTTP by default because they leak internal details — you opt in with `management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info` in properties. Exposing everything in production is a genuine security mistake, so practise being deliberate about it now.

This is a deep-dive because your college projects won't need it. But mentioning "I added Actuator health checks" when describing a deployed project tells an interviewer you've thought past `localhost` — a small line that quietly separates you from most freshers.
tips: Remember one endpoint perfectly: /actuator/health. It's the one production actually depends on.
Exposing /actuator/env publicly can leak secrets — say this in an interview and you'll stand out.
Actuator + one screenshot of /actuator/metrics makes your project README look production-aware.

resources.title: Spring Boot Actuator Tutorial
Spring Boot Actuator in Hindi - Health & Metrics
Spring Boot Actuator — endpoints and configuration

modules.title: REST APIs with Spring Boot
modules.description: Turn your Spring Boot skeleton into a real API — endpoints, JSON in and out, validation, and error handling that behaves the way company codebases expect.

topics.title: How the web talks: HTTP and REST
topics.summary_md:
Before writing an API, you need the conversation it's built on. Every interaction on the web is one **request** and one **response**, and the shape never changes.

Picture the canteen counter at lunch. You hand over an order slip: what you want ("one meals"), and how you want it. The counter hands back a plate and a token — or says "finished, try the next counter." That's HTTP. Your request carries a **method** (the verb: GET to read, POST to create, PUT to replace, DELETE to remove), a **path** (`/api/students/42` — which thing you're talking about), optional **headers**, and sometimes a **body**. The response carries a **status code** (the token: 200 means done, 404 means no such thing, 500 means the kitchen caught fire) and usually a body — for APIs, almost always JSON.

REST is not a technology; it's a set of conventions for arranging this conversation around *resources* (nouns like students, events, orders) instead of actions. So you expose `GET /api/events` rather than `/getAllEventsNow`. One more property matters: HTTP is **stateless** — the server doesn't remember your previous request, so each request must carry everything needed to answer it.

Spend time here even if it feels theoretical. Every Spring annotation in the next topics maps one-to-one onto a piece of the request. When you know the raw conversation, the framework stops being magic.
tips: GET requests should never change data — interviewers probe this with 'can I delete a record with GET?' Technically yes, correctly never.
URLs name nouns, methods supply verbs: POST /api/students, not /api/createStudent.
'Why is HTTP stateless?' is a common warm-up — mention that it's why tokens get sent with every request.

resources.title: What is a REST API? Explained Simply
What is an API? in Hindi - Simple Explanation
API Explained in Tamil - What is REST API?
An overview of HTTP — MDN

topics.title: Your first endpoints: @RestController, GET and POST
topics.summary_md:
This is where your Spring Boot project starts answering the outside world. A **controller** is a class that receives HTTP requests and returns responses; in Spring you mark it `@RestController`, and whatever your methods return gets converted to JSON automatically (Jackson does this behind the scenes — no manual conversion).

Let's build something concrete: a notice-board API for campus events. Create `EventController`, annotate it `@RestController` and `@RequestMapping("/api/events")`. Add a method with `@GetMapping` that returns a `List<Event>` — hit `http://localhost:8080/api/events` in the browser and there's your JSON array. Add another method with `@PostMapping` that takes a new event and adds it to the list. Two annotations, and the fest coordinator can now read and publish events through your API.

A few things to understand rather than copy. `@RequestMapping` at class level sets the base path so you don't repeat `/api/events` on every method. `@GetMapping`/`@PostMapping` are just shortcuts for `@RequestMapping(method = ...)`. And that `Event` you return is a plain Java class with fields and getters — Jackson reads the getters to build the JSON keys.

Keep controllers thin: they receive, delegate to a `@Service`, and return. Business logic in controllers is the single most common code-review comment freshers get in their first job, so build the habit while the projects are small.
tips: @Controller returns view pages; @RestController returns data. Mixing them up is a classic fresher slip.
No getters on your class = empty JSON response. Jackson reads getters, not fields.
Say 'controller thin, service fat' in an interview and be ready to show it in your project.

resources.title: Building a RESTful Web Service — official Spring guide
REST API with Spring Boot - GET and POST Mapping
Spring Boot REST API in Hindi - Controllers & Mappings

topics.title: Path variables vs query params
topics.summary_md:
Two different ways for data to ride along in a URL, and interviewers love checking whether you know which is which.

Think of catching a city bus. The **route number** — 47A — identifies *which bus*. That's a path variable: it's part of the identity, baked into the path itself. Then you have your *preferences*: after 5 pm, express only, window seat if possible. Those are filters that narrow choices without changing which route you're on — that's what query params do.

In URL form: `GET /api/students/17` — the `17` identifies one specific student, so it's a **path variable**, captured with `@PathVariable`. `GET /api/students?year=3&dept=cse` — you're still asking for the students collection, just filtered, so `year` and `dept` are **query params**, captured with `@RequestParam`.

The rule of thumb: if removing it makes the URL meaningless, it belongs in the path. `/api/students/` with no id points at nothing. But `/api/students` without `?year=3` still makes sense — you just get everyone.

On the Spring side, both annotations bind straight into method parameters: `public Student getOne(@PathVariable Long id)` and `public List<Student> search(@RequestParam(required = false) Integer year)`. Note the `required = false` — a query param is usually optional, and forgetting this gives your callers a confusing 400 error when they omit it. Types convert automatically; if someone passes `abc` where a number is expected, Spring returns 400 before your code even runs.
tips: Identity in the path, filters in the query string — one sentence that answers a whole interview question.
@RequestParam is required by default; forgetting required=false is the top cause of surprise 400s.
Mismatched names — @PathVariable("id") vs {studentId} in the mapping — fails at runtime, not compile time. Double-check the spelling.

resources.title: Path Variable and Request Param in Spring Boot
@PathVariable vs @RequestParam in Hindi
Spring @RequestParam Annotation

topics.title: Request bodies, DTOs, and validation
topics.summary_md:
GET requests carry everything in the URL. But when a client *creates* something — a signup, a new event — the data arrives as JSON in the **request body**, and `@RequestBody` tells Spring to convert that JSON into a Java object for you.

Which object, though? Resist the urge to reuse your database entity everywhere. Create a **DTO** (data transfer object) — a plain class shaped exactly like the incoming JSON, nothing more. `StudentSignupRequest` with `name`, `email`, `year`. DTOs keep your API's shape independent from your database's shape, so changing one doesn't silently break the other.

Now, validation. Never trust incoming data — clients send empty names, emails without @, year 99. Think of the hostel admission counter: the warden checks the form *while you're standing there* and hands it straight back if the name field is blank. She doesn't file it and discover the problem next month. Your API should behave the same way: reject bad input at the door, before any logic runs.

Spring makes this declarative. Add `spring-boot-starter-validation`, put constraints on the DTO fields — `@NotBlank(message = "name is required")`, `@Email`, `@Min(1) @Max(4)` — then mark the controller parameter `@Valid @RequestBody StudentSignupRequest req`. Invalid input now triggers a 400 response automatically, with your messages, and your service code only ever sees clean data. Write the constraint messages like you'd want to read them: say what's wrong and what good input looks like.
tips: @NotBlank for strings, @NotNull for objects and numbers — using @NotNull on a string lets "   " through.
Validation lives on the DTO, but nothing happens without @Valid on the controller parameter. Forgetting it is the #1 'why isn't this working' here.
'Why DTOs instead of entities in the API?' — decoupling and not leaking internal fields. Two words interviewers want: separation, security.

resources.title: Request Body and Validation in Spring Boot
Spring Boot Validation in Hindi - @Valid, @NotNull, @Email
Validation in Spring Boot

topics.title: Status codes and clean error handling
topics.summary_md:
A good API tells the truth about what happened, and status codes are its vocabulary. Get these right and frontend developers (including future you) will thank you.

Picture the college library counter, four situations. You ask for a book and get it: **200 OK**. You donate a new book and it's added to the catalogue: **201 Created**. You ask for a book that isn't in the catalogue: **404 Not Found** — nothing broke, the thing just doesn't exist. You hand over a slip with no book name at all: **400 Bad Request** — *your* slip was the problem. And if the librarian's computer crashes mid-search, that's **500 Internal Server Error** — the library's fault, not yours. The families matter more than individual codes: 2xx success, 4xx the client messed up, 5xx the server messed up.

In Spring, returning a plain object gives 200 by default. For anything else, return `ResponseEntity`: `ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED).body(saved)` or `ResponseEntity.notFound().build()`.

But don't scatter try-catch blocks through every controller. Spring gives you one clean pattern: a class marked `@RestControllerAdvice` with `@ExceptionHandler` methods. Your service throws `StudentNotFoundException`; the advice class catches it *anywhere in the app* and turns it into a tidy 404 with a JSON error body. Controllers stay clean, error responses stay consistent, and there's exactly one place to change the error format. This pattern appears in nearly every company codebase, so learn it as the default, not as an extra.
tips: Returning 200 with an error message in the body is the classic fresher API smell — the status line must tell the truth.
404 vs 400 trips people: 404 = the resource doesn't exist, 400 = the request itself is malformed.
'How do you handle exceptions globally in Spring?' — the answer is @RestControllerAdvice, and it's asked a lot.

resources.title: Exception Handling in Spring Boot REST API
HTTP Status Codes & Exception Handling in Hindi
HTTP response status codes — MDN
Error Handling for REST with Spring

topics.title: Documenting your API with Swagger (deep-dive)
topics.summary_md:
Your API works. Now a friend building the frontend asks: "what endpoints do you have, and what JSON does each one want?" WhatsApp-ing screenshots of your controller gets old after the second change — and forgetting to update the message after the third change gets worse. APIs need documentation that can't drift out of date.

That's what **springdoc-openapi** gives you. Add one dependency (`springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui`), restart, and open `http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html`. There's a live web page listing every endpoint in your app — paths, methods, parameters, request shapes — generated by reading your actual controllers. Because it's derived from code, it updates itself when the code changes. Your friend can even press "Try it out" and call your API straight from the page, no Postman needed.

Under the hood, the dependency generates an **OpenAPI specification** — a standard JSON description of your API — and Swagger UI is just a viewer for it. That distinction is worth one sentence in an interview: OpenAPI is the spec, Swagger UI is the tool.

You can polish the docs with annotations like `@Operation(summary = "...")` on endpoints, but honestly, the zero-effort default is already useful. This is a deep-dive topic: skip it if the placement clock is ticking, but adding Swagger to your project takes ten minutes and makes your demo noticeably more professional — a live API page beats a README table every time.
tips: One dependency, zero config, instant docs — there's no excuse for an undocumented project API.
Know the words: OpenAPI is the specification, Swagger UI is the viewer.
In project demos, open swagger-ui.html instead of Postman — interviewers see the whole API at a glance.

resources.title: Swagger / OpenAPI Documentation in Spring Boot
Spring Boot Swagger UI Setup in Hindi
Documenting a Spring REST API Using OpenAPI 3.0

modules.title: HTML/CSS/JS Essentials
modules.description: The front half of full-stack: build real pages, style them properly, and make them talk to the Spring Boot API you just wrote.

topics.title: HTML structure and semantic tags
topics.summary_md:
HTML is not a programming language — it's structure. Every page is a tree of elements, and your first job is to build that tree so it *means* something, not just looks right.

Think of how you fill an exam answer sheet. Name and roll number go in the printed box at the top, answers go in the ruled middle, and "checked by" sits at the bottom. Nobody told you to do this today; the sheet's *structure* carries the meaning. A web page works the same way: `<header>` for the top strip with your site name and navigation (`<nav>`), `<main>` for the actual content, `<section>` and `<article>` to group related pieces, `<footer>` for the closing details.

Here's the fresher trap: you *can* build an entire page out of `<div>` tags, and it will look identical. So why bother with semantic tags? Three real reasons. Screen readers use them to let blind users jump straight to navigation or main content. Search engines use them to understand what your page is about. And developers (including you, three weeks later) read `<nav>` instantly, while `<div class="dv2">` needs archaeology.

Get comfortable with the everyday elements too: headings `<h1>`–`<h6>` (one `<h1>` per page, and never pick a heading level for its font size), paragraphs, lists, links, images with `alt` text, tables for actual tabular data. Then build one page by hand — your own profile page is the classic first target — before touching any CSS.
tips: 'Why semantic tags over divs?' is a genuine interview question — accessibility, SEO, readability. Say all three.
Never choose h3 because it 'looks the right size' — headings are structure; size is CSS's job.
Missing alt text on images is the fastest way to fail an accessibility review.

resources.title: HTML Tutorial for Beginners in Hindi (One Video)
HTML Full Course in Tamil for Beginners
HTML: HyperText Markup Language — MDN

topics.title: Forms and inputs: collecting real data
topics.summary_md:
Almost everything people *do* on the web goes through a form: signups, searches, payments, job applications. Forms are also where HTML meets your backend — the data a form collects is exactly what your Spring Boot API receives — so this topic pays off twice.

Build one properly, start to finish: a registration form for your college fest. A `<form>` element wrapping the fields. `<input type="text">` for the name. `<input type="email">` for email — on a phone this even switches the keyboard to show the @ key. A `<select>` with `<option>`s for department, radio buttons for year (only one choice allowed), checkboxes for which events they'll join (multiple allowed), and a `<button type="submit">` that says exactly what happens: "Register".

Two details separate careful work from copy-paste work. First, **labels**: every input gets a `<label for="...">` matched to the input's `id`. It's not decoration — clicking the label focuses the input, and screen readers depend on the pairing. Second, **built-in validation**: `required`, `min`, `max`, `maxlength`, and the type itself (`email`, `number`) let the browser catch obvious mistakes before anything is sent.

One mental shift to make now: browser validation is a convenience, not a defence. Anyone can bypass it with dev tools, which is exactly why your Spring Boot API validates again with `@Valid`. Client-side checks are for user experience; server-side checks are for truth. Both, always.
tips: Every input needs a label — clickable, screen-reader-friendly, and it makes tap targets bigger on mobile.
input type=email and required give you free validation — use them before writing any JavaScript.
Interview angle: 'client-side validation is UX, server-side validation is security.' One line, big impression.

resources.title: HTML Forms Complete Tutorial in Hindi
HTML Forms in Tamil - Inputs, Labels & Validation
The form element — MDN reference

topics.title: CSS selectors and the box model
topics.summary_md:
CSS answers two questions: *which* elements am I styling (selectors), and *how much space* does each one take (the box model). Get these two right and most "why does my page look broken" moments disappear.

Selectors first. `p` targets every paragraph, `.card` targets elements with `class="card"`, `#signup` targets the one element with that id. You'll combine them: `.card p` means paragraphs *inside* cards. When two rules fight over the same element, **specificity** decides: id beats class, class beats element. When you're staring at a style that refuses to apply, the answer is almost always a more specific rule elsewhere — open dev tools, inspect the element, and read which rule won.

Now the box model. Every element on a page is a rectangular box with four layers. Picture a notice pinned on the department notice board: the printed text is the **content**; the white space between the text and the paper's edge is **padding**; the paper's edge itself is the **border**; and the gap the pinner leaves so it doesn't touch the next notice is **margin**. Every element — heading, button, image — is that notice.

The classic surprise: by default, `width: 200px` sets only the *content* width, so padding and border make the visible box bigger than 200px. That's why nearly every real stylesheet starts with `* { box-sizing: border-box; }` — it makes `width` mean the full visible box, the way humans expect. Adopt it from day one and skip years of confusion.
tips: Set box-sizing: border-box globally in your first line of CSS — everyone does, for good reason.
Padding is inside the border, margin is outside — if you can't say this instantly, redraw the notice.
When a style won't apply, don't add !important — inspect in dev tools and find which rule is winning.

resources.title: CSS Tutorial in Hindi - Selectors and Box Model
CSS Full Course in Tamil for Beginners
box-sizing — MDN reference

topics.title: Flexbox: arranging things without pain
topics.summary_md:
Before flexbox, centering a box on a page was a running joke among developers. Now it's three lines. Flexbox is how you arrange elements in rows and columns — and it's the layout tool you'll reach for daily.

The model: mark a container `display: flex`, and its direct children line up along a **main axis** (a row by default; `flex-direction: column` makes it vertical). Two properties then control placement, and mixing them up is *the* flexbox beginner error. `justify-content` positions children along the main axis; `align-items` positions them across it. Row direction? Then justify-content is horizontal and align-items is vertical. So the famous perfect-centering recipe is: `display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;` on the parent.

Build the classic to make it stick: a navbar for your college club's site. Logo on the left, links on the right — `display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center;` on the nav, and you're done. That `space-between` (first child at one end, last at the other, space distributed between) powers half the headers on the internet.

The pieces you'll use next: `gap` for spacing between children (stop using margins for this), `flex-wrap: wrap` to let items flow to the next line on small screens, and `flex: 1` on a child to let it stretch and fill leftover space. Add one `@media (max-width: 600px)` query switching your nav to `flex-direction: column`, and you've met responsive design too — same boxes, rearranged for a phone.
tips: justify-content = main axis, align-items = cross axis. Tattoo it somewhere.
Flexbox properties go on the parent container, not the children — the most common beginner mistake.
Play Flexbox Froggy once; 'how do you center a div' still gets asked in fresher interviews.

resources.title: CSS Flexbox Tutorial in Hindi
Flexbox in Tamil - Complete Guide with Examples
Basic concepts of flexbox — MDN

topics.title: JavaScript, the DOM, and events
topics.summary_md:
HTML gives you a page; JavaScript makes it *react*. The browser turns your HTML into a live tree of objects called the **DOM**, and JS can read and rewrite that tree at any moment — no reload needed. Every interactive thing you've ever clicked works this way.

The workflow is always the same three steps: **find** an element, **listen** for something happening to it, **change** the page in response. Build the simplest honest version: an attendance counter. One heading showing `0`, one button saying "Mark present". The JS: `document.querySelector("h1")` finds the heading, `button.addEventListener("click", ...)` listens, and inside the handler you increment a variable and set `heading.textContent = count`. Click, click, click — the number climbs without the page ever reloading. That loop — find, listen, change — is 80% of frontend JavaScript.

Your finding tool is `querySelector`, and it takes the *same selectors you learned in CSS* — `".card"`, `"#signup"`, `"form input"` — one skill, two uses. For changing things: `textContent` for text, `classList.add/remove/toggle` for switching styles (define a `.hidden` class in CSS, toggle it in JS — cleaner than editing styles directly), and `element.value` for reading what's typed in an input.

Two habits from day one. Put your `<script>` tag at the end of `<body>` (or use `defer`) so elements exist before your code looks for them — `querySelector` returning `null` is almost always this. And keep the browser console open; `console.log` is your best friend until DevTools feels like home.
tips: querySelector returned null? Your script ran before the HTML loaded. Move it to the end of body or add defer.
Change styles by toggling classes, not by setting element.style line by line — keep design in CSS.
addEventListener('click', handler) — pass the function itself, don't call it with (). Writing handler() runs it immediately, once.

resources.title: JavaScript DOM Manipulation in Hindi (One Shot)
JavaScript DOM and Events in Tamil
Introduction to the DOM — MDN

topics.title: fetch: your page talks to your API
tips: fetch doesn't reject on 404/500 — always check res.ok. This one line prevents hours of confusion.
await res.json() — forgetting the await gives you a Promise instead of data, and 'undefined' everywhere.
First CORS error? You're officially a full-stack developer. @CrossOrigin on the controller while learning; proper config later.
