Using the Fetch API — MDN

modules.title: React Essentials
modules.description: The frontend half of your stack. Learn just enough React to build real screens: components, props, state, and fetching data from your own API.

topics.title: Why React exists (and what a component is)
topics.summary_md:
Before React, building a page meant writing HTML and then writing separate JavaScript to poke at it every time data changed. That works for one button. It falls apart for a real app.

React's idea is simple: your UI is a function of your data. You describe *what the screen should look like* for the current data, and React figures out what to update when the data changes. You never manually hunt for an element and change its text.

A **component** is a JavaScript function that returns a piece of UI. Small pieces compose into big screens.

Concrete example: your college fest website has 30 event cards — same layout, different event name, venue, and time. Without React you'd copy-paste the same HTML 30 times, and fixing one spacing bug means fixing it 30 times. With React you write one `EventCard` component and render it 30 times with different data. One fix, everywhere.

This "describe the UI, feed it data" mindset is the whole game. JSX, props, and state (the next topics) are just the grammar for it. Set up a project with Vite (`npm create vite@latest`), delete the demo code, and render your own component before moving on. Ten minutes of doing beats an hour of watching.
tips: Don't start with a huge tutorial — create a Vite project today and render one component with your name in it.
Interviewers ask 'why React over plain JavaScript?' — the answer is reusable components and automatic UI updates, not 'it's popular'.
A component is just a function. If you can write a function, you can write a component.

resources.title: React Tutorial for Beginners - What is React and Why Learn It
React JS Tutorial in Hindi - Introduction and Setup
React — Quick Start

topics.title: JSX and props: passing data into components
topics.summary_md: JSX looks like HTML written inside JavaScript, and that's basically what it is — with one superpower: curly braces. Write `{studentName}` inside your markup and React drops the value in. A few renames to remember: `class` becomes `className`, and every tag must close (``). **Props** are how a parent component hands data to a child — like function arguments, because that's literally what they are. Concrete example: your college ID card. Every student's card has the same layout — photo on the left, name, department, roll number. The *layout* is the component; your *details* are the props. `` and `` reuse one design for every student. Two patterns you'll use daily. Conditional rendering: `{isHosteller ? : }` shows different UI for different data. Lists: `students.map(s => )` turns an array into a column of cards — and React wants a `key` on each item so it can track them when the list changes. One rule to tattoo somewhere: **props flow down, and a child never modifies its props.** Data goes parent → child. When something needs to *change*, that's state — next topic.
tips: Forgetting `key` when rendering a list with map is the most common beginner warning — use a stable id, not the array index.
Props are read-only. If you're trying to change a prop inside a child, your design is wrong — lift it to state in the parent.
Interview one-liner: 'JSX is syntax sugar that compiles to JavaScript function calls' — saying that cleanly earns points.

resources.title: React JSX and Props Explained for Beginners
React in Tamil - Components and Props for Beginners
React — Passing Props to a Component

topics.title: State with useState: data that changes
topics.summary_md:
Props come from outside and don't change. **State** is a component's own memory — data that changes while someone uses your app, where the screen must update to match.

The hook: `const [count, setCount] = useState(0)`. You get the current value and a setter. Call `setCount(count + 1)` and React re-renders the component with the new value. That re-render is the magic — and the rule that follows from it trips up every beginner: **never write `count++` or assign to state directly.** A plain variable changes in memory, but React has no idea it happened, so the screen stays frozen. Only the setter tells React "something changed, redraw."

Concrete example: the canteen token counter. The aunty at the counter taps a button for each samosa order. `orders` is state; the tap calls `setOrders(orders + 1)`; the display updates instantly. Now add a second piece of state, `isSoldOut`, and render "Sold out, come tomorrow" when it's true — two `useState` calls, one honest little app.

Where should state live? In the **closest common parent** of every component that needs it. The parent owns the state and passes it down as props — that's called lifting state up, and it answers half the React questions you'll face in interviews.

Build the counter yourself. Then add a reset button. That's the whole concept, earned.
tips: Updating state with `count++` instead of `setCount` is the #1 beginner bug — the value changes but the screen doesn't.
State updates are asynchronous: logging state right after calling the setter shows the old value. That's expected, not a bug.
Classic interview question: 'props vs state' — props come from the parent and are read-only; state is owned by the component and changes over time.

resources.title: useState Hook in React - Complete Beginner Tutorial
React State aur useState Hook in Hindi
React — State: A Component's Memory

topics.title: useEffect and fetch: getting data from a server
tips: Forgetting the `[]` dependency array creates an infinite fetch loop — if your network tab is going crazy, check this first.
Always render something for loading and error — hostel Wi-Fi will find your missing error state on demo day.
`res.json()` also returns a promise — that's why there are two `.then`s. Interviewers love asking why.

resources.title: useEffect Hook and Fetching API Data in React
React useEffect and API Call in Tamil
React — Synchronizing with Effects
MDN — Using the Fetch API

topics.title: Pages without page reloads: SPA routing
topics.summary_md:
Click a link on a normal website and the browser throws away the whole page and loads a new one. Click a link in Gmail and the screen changes instantly with no white flash. That second experience is a **single-page application (SPA)**: the browser loads one page, and JavaScript swaps components in and out as you navigate. React apps are SPAs by default.

The tool for this in React is a router (the common library is React Router). You declare which URL shows which component: `/` shows the notice list, `/notices/12` shows one notice, `/post` shows the form. The router reads the URL, renders the matching component, and updates the URL when you navigate — all without a reload.

Concrete example: your department's exam-results portal. Old style, every click on "semester 3" reloads the entire site — header, footer, everything — just to change a table. SPA style, only the table swaps, instantly. Same data, respectful of a slow connection.

Two things to know: use the router's `Link` component instead of `<a>` tags (an `<a>` triggers a real reload and defeats the purpose), and URL params like `/notices/:id` let one component render different data based on the URL.

This is a nice-to-have for the mini project — one screen is fine there — but most real frontends you'll touch at work use it.
tips: Using `<a href>` instead of the router's `Link` silently causes full page reloads — a classic 'why is my app slow' bug.
Interview angle: know what an SPA is and its trade-off — fast navigation, but the first load is heavier.
Don't memorize router APIs — know the concept and read the docs when you need them.

resources.title: React Router Tutorial - Single Page Application Routing
React Router in Hindi - SPA Navigation Tutorial
MDN — SPA (Single-page application) glossary entry

modules.title: Full-Stack Mini Project
modules.description: Build one real thing end to end: a campus notice board with a Spring Boot API and a React frontend, connected, deployed, and ready for your resume.

topics.title: Pick the scope, sketch the screens, write the API contract
topics.summary_md:
Every unfinished student project died the same way: the scope grew before the first version worked. So we fix the scope now and refuse to move it.

The project: a **campus notice board**. Anyone can read notices; there's a form to post one; a notice can be deleted. That's it. No login, no comments, no likes — version 1 earns the right to exist by shipping.

Concrete example of why this matters: think of the actual notice board outside your department office. It does three things — you read notices, someone pins one up, old ones get taken down. It doesn't have user profiles. Neither does your v1.

Do three things on paper before touching code. First, sketch the two screens: a list of notice cards, and a small posting form above it. Ugly pencil sketches are fine. Second, decide the data: a notice has `id`, `title`, `body`, `postedBy`, `createdAt`. Third — the most professional habit in this whole module — write the **API contract**: `GET /api/notices` returns a JSON array, `POST /api/notices` accepts `{title, body, postedBy}` and returns the created notice, `DELETE /api/notices/{id}` removes one.

With the contract written down, backend and frontend become two independent tasks that meet in the middle. This is exactly how teams at real companies split work, and saying so in an interview lands well.
tips: Write the API contract before any code — it's the difference between a project and a mess.
Feature creep kills student projects. Write 'v2 ideas' in a separate note and don't touch them until v1 is deployed.
Keep your sketches and contract — walking an interviewer through 'here's how I planned it' is a story most freshers can't tell.

resources.title: How to Plan a Full Stack Project - Beginner to Deployment
Full Stack Project Planning in Hindi - Kaise Start Kare
MDN — HTTP request methods

topics.title: Build the backend: a Spring Boot REST API
topics.summary_md:
Time to build the server side of the contract you wrote. Three layers, each with one job:

- **Controller** (`NoticeController`) — receives HTTP requests, returns JSON. `@GetMapping("/api/notices")`, `@PostMapping`, `@DeleteMapping("/{id}")`.
- **Service** (`NoticeService`) — the rules. Today it's thin (reject a notice with an empty title); it exists so logic never leaks into controllers.
- **Repository** (`NoticeRepository extends JpaRepository`) — talks to the database. Spring Data writes the SQL for you.

Start at [start.spring.io](https://start.spring.io) with Web, Spring Data JPA, and H2. H2 is an in-memory database — zero setup, perfect for building. Your `Notice` entity's fields become table columns through JPA annotations.

Here's the moment this becomes real: run the app, open a terminal, and post an actual notice —

```
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/notices \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"title":"Lost calculator in Lab 2","body":"Casio fx-991, name scratched on back","postedBy":"Divya, ECE"}'
```

Then `GET /api/notices` and see it come back. No frontend exists yet, and your API already works — that's the payoff of the contract-first plan.

Test every endpoint with curl or Postman before moving on, including the sad paths: empty title should return 400, deleting an id that doesn't exist should return 404. Freshers skip the sad paths; interviewers ask about them.
tips: Return proper status codes: 201 for created, 400 for a bad request, 404 for not found — 'everything returns 200' is a code-review classic.
Test with curl or Postman after every endpoint, not after all three — small feedback loops save hours.
Interviewers ask 'why separate controller and service?' — answer: controllers handle HTTP, services hold business rules, so rules stay testable.

resources.title: Spring Boot REST API Tutorial - CRUD from Scratch
Spring Boot CRUD API in Tamil for Beginners
Spring — Building a RESTful Web Service (official guide)
Baeldung — Spring Boot Tutorial: Bootstrap a Simple Application

topics.title: Build the frontend: React screens for the notice board
topics.summary_md:
Now the React side — and here's a trick that keeps you moving: **build against fake data first.** Hardcode an array of three notices in your code and get the whole UI working before you touch the network. Wiring comes next topic; today is pure Module 10 practice.

Break the screen into components, the way you'd break a big problem into functions:

- `App` — owns the `notices` state (start with the fake array).
- `NoticeList` — receives notices as a prop, maps them into cards.
- `NoticeCard` — one notice: title, body, who posted it, a delete button.
- `NoticeForm` — controlled inputs for title, body, and name.

Concrete example of the data flow you're building: someone types "Selling my drafter, final years DM me" into the form and hits Post. The form doesn't add the notice itself — it calls a function passed down from `App`, `App` updates state, and the new card appears in the list. One direction: state lives at the top, events bubble up, data flows down. Delete works the same way in reverse.

A **controlled input** means the input's value comes from state (`value={title} onChange={...}`) — React owns what's in the box, which makes clearing the form after posting a one-liner.

Don't chase beauty yet. Readable cards, a form that works, delete that deletes. Working and plain beats pretty and broken — you can style after the wiring holds.
tips: Build with hardcoded data first — debugging UI and network code at the same time is how beginners get stuck for days.
If a form input ignores your typing, you set `value` without `onChange` — the most common controlled-input bug.
Name components after what they show (`NoticeCard`, not `Card2`) — you'll thank yourself when explaining the code in an interview.

resources.title: React CRUD App Tutorial for Beginners - Full Project
React Project in Hindi - Components, State aur Forms
React — Thinking in React

topics.title: Connect the two: fetch, CORS, and honest error states
topics.summary_md:
Both halves work alone. Time to introduce them — and to meet the error every full-stack fresher meets on this exact day.

Replace the fake array: in `App`, a `useEffect` fetches `GET http://localhost:8080/api/notices` on load and puts the result in state. Open the browser… and the console goes red: *"blocked by CORS policy."*

Don't panic — nothing is broken. Your React dev server runs on `localhost:3000` (or 5173) and your API on `localhost:8080`. Those are different **origins**, and the browser refuses to hand one origin's JavaScript another origin's response unless the *server* says it's fine. It's a security rule protecting real people, not a bug in your code. Think of it like the hostel gate register: the watchman doesn't stop you because he suspects you — visitors from another hostel just need the warden's note. The fix is that note: `@CrossOrigin(origins = "http://localhost:3000")` on your controller (or a global CORS config), telling the browser your frontend is a welcome guest.

Once data flows, wire the rest: the form POSTs and appends the created notice to state; delete calls DELETE and removes the card. Then make it honest — a loading line while fetching, a readable message with a retry when the API is down, and "No notices yet. Post the first one →" when the board is empty.

When you can post a notice from the browser and watch it land in the database, stop and enjoy it. That round trip is the whole job description.
tips: CORS is enforced by the browser and fixed on the server — a config change in Spring, not a React fix. Interviewers love this question.
Kill your backend on purpose and look at the app — if the screen goes blank instead of showing an error, fix that before demo day.
After a successful POST, update state with the notice the server returned (it has the real id) — don't re-add the form's local copy.

resources.title: Connect React Frontend to Spring Boot Backend - Fix CORS
React aur Backend API Connect Karna in Hindi - CORS Error Fix
MDN — Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)

topics.title: Ship it: deploy both halves and write a README that sells it
topics.summary_md:
A project on your laptop is a story. A project with a URL is proof. Recruiters and seniors click links; nobody clones your repo and runs two servers to evaluate you.

The free path: frontend on **Vercel** (connect the GitHub repo, it builds and deploys on every push), backend on **Render's** free tier (it runs your Spring Boot jar and gives you a public URL). Swap H2 for a free Postgres so notices survive restarts. Two things change in your code: the frontend's API URL must come from an environment variable instead of a hardcoded `localhost:8080`, and your CORS config must allow your Vercel domain. Real values like database passwords live in the host's environment settings — never in the repo. Anything inside your React bundle ships to every visitor's browser, so secrets only ever live on the backend.

One honest warning so you don't lose an interview to it: free backend tiers sleep when idle, and the first request can take ~30 seconds to wake. Open your app five minutes before any demo.

Concrete example of the payoff: in a campus drive HR asks, "any projects?" One student describes a college mini project from a lab manual. You slide a link: a live notice board, posting works, and the README below shows a screenshot, the API endpoints, and a line on how you fixed CORS. Same question, different league.

README structure: what it does (two lines), live links, screenshot, tech stack, API table, run-locally steps, and "what I'd build next."
tips: Hardcoded `localhost:8080` in fetch calls is the #1 reason deployed frontends show a blank list — use an environment variable.
Free backends sleep — open the app before your interview slot so the first click isn't a 30-second wait.
A README with a screenshot and live link gets read; a bare auto-generated one gets closed. Ten minutes, big return.

resources.title: Deploy Full Stack App Free - React on Vercel, Spring Boot on Render
Website Deploy Kaise Kare in Hindi - Free Hosting Tutorial
Baeldung — Properties with Spring and Spring Boot (externalized config)

modules.title: Fresher Interview Prep
modules.description: Everything between you and the offer letter: a resume that gets shortlisted, the core CS questions every panel asks, and calm answers for the HR round.

topics.title: A one-page resume that earns the shortlist
topics.summary_md:
A recruiter at a campus drive spends under 30 seconds on your resume. Your job is to survive those 30 seconds — nothing more. That changes what goes on the page.

One page. Order for a fresher: name and links (GitHub, LinkedIn, live project URL) → skills → projects → education → certifications if any. Projects above education, because your notice board says more about you than your seventh-semester GPA.

Each project gets two or three bullets shaped like *what you built + the tech + one specific number or detail*. Watch the difference:

- Weak: "Made a website using web technologies as part of coursework."
- Strong: "Built a campus notice board — Spring Boot REST API (3 endpoints, Postgres) with a React frontend; deployed on Render and Vercel."

Same student, same project. The second one gives the interviewer three follow-up questions to ask you — and follow-ups you can answer are exactly how fresher interviews go well.

Rules that save you from silent rejection: no photo, no "objective" paragraph, no skill bars showing "Java 80%", and never list a skill you can't answer basics on — writing "Docker" invites a Docker question. Keep it to what you can defend.

Export as PDF, name it `YourName_Resume.pdf`, and get one senior or friend to read it cold. If they can't say what you built within 20 seconds, rewrite the bullets.
tips: Every skill on your resume is an invitation for a question — list only what you can defend.
'Team player with strong communication skills' says nothing; a live project link says everything. Spend words on specifics.
ATS software reads your resume before humans do — plain single-column layout beats a fancy two-column design.

resources.title: Resume for Freshers - What Actually Gets Shortlisted
Resume Kaise Banaye - Freshers ke liye Complete Guide
Resume Tips in Tamil for Freshers - IT Jobs

topics.title: The core CS questions every panel asks
topics.summary_md:
Fresher technical rounds are more predictable than your university exams. Across service and product companies, the same core keeps appearing: OOP, SQL, and a light layer of OS and networks. Prepare this set deliberately and you walk in knowing most of the paper.

**OOP (asked almost every time):** the four pillars — encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction — each with *your own* example, not the textbook's. Concrete example that works in interviews: UPI apps. You tap Pay and money moves; you never see or touch the bank's internal transaction steps — the complexity is hidden behind a simple interface (abstraction), and the balance can't be modified except through controlled operations (encapsulation). An example from an app the panel uses daily lands better than `class Animal`. Also be ready for: interface vs abstract class, method overloading vs overriding, and why Java doesn't support multiple inheritance of classes.

**SQL:** write a SELECT with WHERE, GROUP BY, and a JOIN by hand. Know primary key vs foreign key vs unique, and what NULL does to comparisons. Panels ask you to write the query on paper — practice on paper at least once.

**OS and networks (lighter):** process vs thread, what a deadlock is, what happens when you type a URL and press Enter, TCP vs UDP in one line each.

Method that sticks: for every question, write a two-line answer plus one example in your own words. Fifteen such cards beat three hours of passive video.
tips: Prepare your own example for each OOP pillar — interviewers can tell a memorized definition from understanding in one follow-up.
'What happens when you type a URL and hit Enter' appears everywhere — prepare a calm 60-second version.
If you don't know, say 'I haven't worked with that, but here's my guess' — bluffing is the only wrong answer.

resources.title: Java Interview Questions for Freshers - OOP Concepts Explained
Technical Interview Questions in Hindi - OOPs, DBMS, OS
Baeldung — Object-Oriented Programming Concepts in Java

topics.title: The HR round: honest answers, prepared calmly
topics.summary_md:
You cleared the technical round — don't lose the offer in the friendly-sounding one. The HR round isn't a chat; it's an evaluation of communication, self-awareness, and whether you'll actually join and stay. The good news: the questions are the most predictable of the whole process, so prepare them like a syllabus.

**"Tell me about yourself"** — 60 to 90 seconds, present → past → future: what you're studying, one project you built (your notice board, with one specific detail), what role you're looking for. Don't recite your resume top to bottom; they're holding it.

**"Strengths and weaknesses"** — a real weakness plus what you're doing about it. "I used to jump into coding without planning; now I write the API contract first" is honest and shows growth. "I'm a perfectionist" is a rehearsed non-answer everyone has heard.

**The uncomfortable ones — arrears, gaps, low CGPA:** answer in three beats: fact, context, forward motion. Concrete example: "Yes, I had a backlog in third semester. Family situation that year; I cleared it the next attempt, and my last three semesters have been my strongest." No excuses, no apologizing twice, done. Panels respect a straight answer far more than a defensive story.

**Relocation and shifts:** decide your real answer *before* the interview, with your family if needed. A fumbled "umm, maybe" reads as a no.

Record yourself answering these once — Fledge's Communication Lab works for exactly this. You'll hear the fillers before an interviewer does.
tips: Prepare 'tell me about yourself' word by word and practice aloud — winging the most predictable question is a choice.
For arrears or gap questions: fact, context, forward motion. Never sound apologetic twice for the same thing.
Ask one genuine question at the end — 'what does a fresher's first month look like here?' beats 'no questions, sir'.

resources.title: HR Interview Questions and Answers for Freshers
HR Interview Tips in Tamil - Tell Me About Yourself
How to Answer HR Interview Questions - Fresher Guide

topics.title: Service vs product companies: same you, different game
topics.summary_md:
TCS and a product startup might interview you in the same campus week, but they're testing different things. Knowing the difference lets you prepare for the game you're actually playing instead of splitting effort blindly.

**Service companies** (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Accenture, Cognizant…) hire in bulk through a standard pipeline: an aptitude and verbal test first (this filters more students than anything technical — do timed mock tests, seriously), then a technical round on core CS — OOP, SQL, one language — plus your project, then HR. Coding, when asked, is usually one or two easy problems: reverse a string, count vowels, simple patterns. Your Module 12 core-CS prep plus your live project covers most of it.

**Product companies** (Zoho, Freshworks, startups, and the big names) filter with DSA instead. Online coding round with easy-to-medium problems, then interviews where you solve on a shared screen and *talk while you think* — approach first, complexity out loud, then code. Projects get grilled deeper too: "why Postgres?", "what breaks if two people post at once?"

Concrete example: two friends from the same class. One targets the TCS NQT — her week is aptitude mocks and SQL-on-paper. The other has a Zoho off-campus test — his week is two DSA problems a day from the practice sets here. Same degree, opposite prep, both correct.

Neither door is "better" for you specifically. A service offer in hand while you keep practicing for product roles is a strong, honest position — momentum beats waiting.
tips: For service companies, aptitude speed eliminates more students than coding — do timed mocks, not just topic practice.
For product interviews, practice saying your approach out loud before coding — silent solving loses points even when correct.
An offer in hand changes everything: accept honestly, keep growing, and don't let anyone shame the service-company route.
